{"id":"holomush-4vrc.87","title":"PolicyEffect lacks validation at DB deserialization boundary in store.scanPolicy","description":"## Type: PolicyEffect (internal/access/policy/types/types.go:41-52)

PolicyEffect is a string type with two valid constants (PolicyEffectPermit, PolicyEffectForbid). In store/postgres.go:47 and :68, raw DB strings are cast directly to PolicyEffect without validation:

    p.Effect = types.PolicyEffect(effect)

If the DB contains a corrupted or unexpected value (e.g. "allow" instead of "permit"), it silently becomes a valid PolicyEffect. The ToEffect() method handles this by mapping unknowns to EffectDefaultDeny (fail-safe), so this is not a security issue.

However, validating at the deserialization boundary would catch DB corruption earlier and produce clearer error messages rather than silently defaulting. A ValidatePolicyEffect(s string) function or a ParsePolicyEffect(s string) (PolicyEffect, error) constructor would make the type's invariant explicit at all entry points.

### Ratings
- Encapsulation: 5/10 -- Underlying string is freely castable, no constructor
- Invariant Expression: 6/10 -- Constants express valid values but nothing prevents invalid ones
- Invariant Usefulness: 7/10 -- ToEffect() fail-safe prevents security issues from bad values
- Invariant Enforcement: 4/10 -- No enforcement at any boundary; relies entirely on ToEffect() fallback

### Suggested Improvement
Add a ParsePolicyEffect function:

    func ParsePolicyEffect(s string) (PolicyEffect, error) {
        switch PolicyEffect(s) {
        case PolicyEffectPermit, PolicyEffectForbid:
            return PolicyEffect(s), nil
        default:
            return "", fmt.Errorf("invalid policy effect: %q", s)
        }
    }

Use it in scanPolicy/scanPolicies instead of the raw cast. This catches DB corruption at the earliest possible point.","status":"open","priority":2,"issue_type":"task","owner":"4678+seanb4t@users.noreply.github.com","created_at":"2026-02-17T00:23:52Z","created_by":"seanb4t","updated_at":"2026-02-17T00:23:52Z","external_ref":"https://github.com/holomush/holomush/pull/88","labels":["aspect:types","pr-review-finding","severity:suggestion","turn:2"]}

{"id":"holomush-4vrc.86","title":"Call site count discrepancy: spec says 25 world call sites, implementation has 26","description":"The Phase 7.6 plan (docs/plans/2026-02-06-full-abac-phase-7.6.md line 90) states '29 production call sites (3 in command, 25 in world, 1 in plugin)'. However, the implementation has 26 world call sites, not 25.

Breakdown per spec:
- Locations: 6 (GetLocation, CreateLocation, UpdateLocation, DeleteLocation, FindLocationByName, ExamineLocation)
- Exits: 5 (GetExit, CreateExit, UpdateExit, DeleteExit, GetExitsByLocation)
- Objects: 6 (GetObject, CreateObject, UpdateObject, DeleteObject, MoveObject, ExamineObject)
- Characters: 5 (GetCharacter, GetCharactersByLocation, MoveCharacter, ExamineCharacter, DeleteCharacter)
- Scenes: 3 (AddSceneParticipant, RemoveSceneParticipant, ListSceneParticipants)
Expected total: 25

Actual implementation (internal/world/service.go checkAccess calls):
- LOCATION entity: 5 calls (lines 135, 155, 184, 213, 935)
- EXIT entity: 5 calls (lines 242, 265, 297, 321, 359)
- OBJECT entity: 5 calls (lines 375, 395, 427, 459, 500)
- CHARACTER entity: 4 calls (lines 559, 588, 610, 697)
- SCENE entity: 3 calls (lines 627, 648, 666)
- EXAMINE entity: 3 calls (lines 794, 852, 907 for Location/Object/Character examine operations)
Actual total: 26

The discrepancy stems from the spec counting 'ExamineLocation', 'ExamineObject', and 'ExamineCharacter' as part of their respective entity categories, but the implementation uses a separate 'EXAMINE' entity prefix for all three examine operations.

Impact: This is a documentation/counting issue, not a functional problem. The implementation is actually more complete than the spec counted.

Suggested fix: Update the spec's call site count from 25 to 26 for world package, and clarify that examine operations use a separate EXAMINE entity prefix. Total production call sites: 30 (not 29).

{"id":"holomush-4vrc.85","title":"Decision type has mixed encapsulation: unexported allowed/effect but exported Policies/Attributes/Reason/PolicyID","description":"## Type: Decision (internal/access/policy/types/types.go:96-103)

The Decision type correctly makes `allowed` and `effect` unexported with accessor methods and enforces their invariant through NewDecision(). However, `Reason`, `PolicyID`, `Policies`, and `Attributes` are exported fields that can be mutated after construction by any consumer.

Currently all post-construction mutations (`decision.Policies = satisfied`, `decision.Attributes = bags`) are confined to engine.go, which is the trusted boundary. But the mixed visibility pattern (some fields private, some public) creates a design inconsistency that could lead to accidental mutation as the codebase grows.

### Ratings
- Encapsulation: 7/10 -- Critical fields protected, but 4 exported mutable fields break the pattern
- Invariant Expression: 8/10 -- Zero-value fail-closed is excellent; Validate() provides runtime check
- Invariant Usefulness: 9/10 -- Prevents the #1 security risk (allowed/effect desync)
- Invariant Enforcement: 7/10 -- Constructor enforces, Validate() checks, but exported fields bypass

### Suggested Improvement
Consider extending NewDecision() or adding a builder to accept Policies and Attributes at construction time, or adding WithPolicies()/WithAttributes() methods that return new Decision values. This would make the entire type consistently immutable after construction.

Alternatively, make Policies/Attributes unexported with getter methods (like allowed/effect). The getter for Policies should return a defensive copy to match GetCapabilities() pattern in CommandEntry.

This is a suggestion-level concern because the current mutation scope is limited to the engine package.","status":"open","priority":2,"issue_type":"task","owner":"4678+seanb4t@users.noreply.github.com","created_at":"2026-02-17T00:23:39Z","created_by":"seanb4t","updated_at":"2026-02-17T00:23:39Z","external_ref":"https://github.com/holomush/holomush/pull/88","labels":["aspect:types","pr-review-finding","severity:suggestion","turn:2"]}

{"id":"holomush-4vrc.93","title":"CRITICAL: decision.Reason and decision.PolicyID used without parentheses — stores func pointer instead of string","description":"**Bug:** decision.Reason and decision.PolicyID are method calls (returning string) but are referenced WITHOUT parentheses in 6 locations. In Go, d.Reason (without parens) evaluates to a bound method value (func() string), not the string itself. When passed to oops.With() or slog, the error context stores a function pointer (e.g., 0x1005e8620) instead of the actual reason/policy_id string.

**Impact:** All error context from denied/failed access decisions is silently corrupted. Debugging access control issues becomes impossible because reason and policy_id in logs and oops errors contain function pointers instead of meaningful strings.

**Affected locations:**
- internal/command/dispatcher.go:208-209  (.With("reason", decision.Reason) and .With("policy_id", decision.PolicyID))
- internal/command/handlers/boot.go:55-56  (same pattern in checkCapability)
- internal/world/service.go:115  (slog call: "policy_id", decision.PolicyID, "reason", decision.Reason)
- internal/world/service.go:119-120  (.With("reason", decision.Reason) and .With("policy_id", decision.PolicyID))
- internal/world/service.go:123  (oops.With on denied path, same issue)

**Fix:** Add parentheses to all 6 locations:
  decision.Reason  → decision.Reason()
  decision.PolicyID → decision.PolicyID()

Verified with test program: decision.Reason evaluates to func() string type, decision.Reason() evaluates to string type.","status":"open","priority":0,"issue_type":"bug","owner":"4678+seanb4t@users.noreply.github.com","created_at":"2026-02-17T00:58:15Z","created_by":"seanb4t","updated_at":"2026-02-17T00:58:15Z","external_ref":"https://github.com/holomush/holomush/pull/88","labels":["aspect:errors","pr-review-finding","severity:critical","turn:3"]}

{"id":"holomush-phq3.4","title":"listCommandsFn returns non-nil error alongside valid result (dead error string)","description":"In internal/plugin/hostfunc/commands.go, listCommandsFn returns two values: a non-nil result table AND a non-nil error string when hadEngineError is true (lines ~128-133 in the new code). This creates an inconsistent API contract where both return values are non-nil simultaneously.

The help/main.lua plugin checks `if result == nil` before checking `err`, so when result is a valid (if incomplete) table, the error string is never read. The error string return in the hadEngineError case is therefore dead code in the current consumer. This will mislead future Lua plugin authors who follow the standard Lua (result, err) convention — they will expect that a non-nil err means result is nil/unusable.

The correct fix is either:
1. Don't return the error string when result is valid (incomplete flag is sufficient signaling) — callers check result.incomplete instead
2. Document clearly in the hostfunc that both can be non-nil simultaneously and update the Lua convention docs

The `incomplete` field on the result table is the correct mechanism for partial results; the redundant error string return just creates confusion. The API contract comment on listCommandsFn says 'Returns: ({commands: [...], incomplete: bool}, error string)' but doesn't specify when error is non-nil alongside a valid result table.","status":"open","priority":1,"issue_type":"bug","owner":"4678+seanb4t@users.noreply.github.com","created_at":"2026-03-10T00:04:00Z","created_by":"seanb4t","updated_at":"2026-03-10T00:04:00Z","external_ref":"https://github.com/holomush/holomush/pull/88"}

{"id":"holomush-phq3.8","title":"hasBypass uses fmt.Errorf instead of oops for infra failure — inconsistent with project conventions","description":"In internal/command/rate_limit_middleware.go, hasBypass() uses fmt.Errorf for infrastructure failures:

  return false, fmt.Errorf("infrastructure failure during bypass check: %s", decision.Reason())

Per CLAUDE.md conventions, all error creation should use oops for structured context. The preferred pattern would be:

  return false, oops.With("reason", decision.Reason()).
      With("policy_id", decision.PolicyID()).
      Errorf("infrastructure failure during bypass check")

This is minor since the error is only logged in Enforce() at Warn level and not returned to callers, but it's inconsistent with the rest of the PR which carefully uses oops throughout all error paths. In particular, decision.PolicyID() is available but not included in the error context.","status":"open","priority":2,"issue_type":"task","owner":"4678+seanb4t@users.noreply.github.com","created_at":"2026-03-10T00:05:54Z","created_by":"seanb4t","updated_at":"2026-03-10T00:05:54Z","external_ref":"https://github.com/holomush/holomush/pull/88"}

{"id":"holomush-phq3.6","title":"checkAccess double-wraps ErrPermissionDenied with redundant oops layer","description":"In internal/world/service.go, checkAccess wraps ErrPermissionDenied with two oops layers:

  deniedErr := oops.With("reason", decision.Reason()).With("policy_id", decision.PolicyID()).Wrap(ErrPermissionDenied)
  return oops.Code(denyCode).Wrap(deniedErr)

This creates a double-nested oops structure. The oops context from the inner Wrap (reason, policy_id) may be shadowed or inaccessible to callers who call oopsErr.Context() on the outer error, depending on how oops traverses the chain.

The preferred pattern per CLAUDE.md conventions would be a single oops call:
  return oops.Code(denyCode).
      With("reason", decision.Reason()).
      With("policy_id", decision.PolicyID()).
      Wrap(ErrPermissionDenied)

This ensures all context is accessible at a single level. The double-wrap pattern is inconsistent with the other return paths in the same function (which use single-chain oops).

File: internal/world/service.go","status":"open","priority":2,"issue_type":"task","owner":"4678+seanb4t@users.noreply.github.com","created_at":"2026-03-10T00:04:17Z","created_by":"seanb4t","updated_at":"2026-03-10T00:04:17Z","external_ref":"https://github.com/holomush/holomush/pull/88"}

{"id":"holomush-phq3.3","title":"AccessRequest struct fields are exported, NewAccessRequest validation can be bypassed","description":"File: internal/access/policy/types/types.go (AccessRequest struct, NewAccessRequest function)

The AccessRequest struct has exported fields (Subject, Action, Resource), meaning any package can construct a zero-value or partial AccessRequest using struct literal syntax without going through the NewAccessRequest() factory that enforces non-empty field validation. This is visible in the test files themselves (e.g., types.AccessRequest{Subject: ..., Action: ..., Resource: ...} in mock expectations).

All production call sites in this PR correctly use NewAccessRequest() or the helper wrappers (CheckCapability, checkAccess), so there is no active bypass. However, the validation contract is not structurally enforced: future code could accidentally construct AccessRequest{} with an empty subject, bypassing access control silently (engine would reject it at NewAccessRequest validation, but only if NewAccessRequest is used).

Suggested fix: Either (a) unexport the struct fields and expose only NewAccessRequest() as the construction path (with accessor methods if needed), or (b) add a Validate() method to AccessRequest and call it at the start of Engine.Evaluate() as a defense-in-depth guard, so even if a struct literal bypasses the factory, the engine rejects it.","status":"open","priority":2,"issue_type":"task","owner":"4678+seanb4t@users.noreply.github.com","created_at":"2026-03-10T00:02:26Z","created_by":"seanb4t","updated_at":"2026-03-10T00:02:26Z","external_ref":"https://github.com/holomush/holomush/pull/88"}

{"id":"holomush-phq3.1","title":"Attribute resolution failure allows evaluation to proceed with partial bags","description":"File: internal/access/policy/engine.go (Step 3 / Step 3b)

When resolver.Resolve() returns both a non-nil error AND a partial bags value, the engine logs a warning but continues evaluation using those partial bags. The staleness check (Step 3b) only denies when the cache is stale; it does not deny when attribute data is incomplete due to a provider failure.

If a policy's conditions depend on attributes (e.g., character.role, character.location_id) that were not populated because a provider errored, the condition evaluation will run against a bags object missing those fields. Depending on how DSL conditions handle missing attributes, a condition that should have been false (and thus denied) could potentially evaluate unexpectedly — or conversely, a deny policy's condition could fail to fire, falling through to a default-deny. The exact impact depends on individual policy condition logic.

The old behavior (line 2423-2426 of diff) replaced the entire bags with NewAttributeBags() on error, ensuring a clean empty state. The new behavior preserves partial bags from successful providers but still proceeds to evaluate policies. This is a deliberate design choice per the diff comment, but the security invariant should be documented and tested: specifically, that partial bags never cause an allow decision when a full bags would deny.

Suggested fix: Either (a) fail-closed on any attribute resolution error (return EffectDefaultDeny with IsInfraFailure=true), or (b) add explicit contract test verifying that partial attribute resolution + permissive policy still results in deny when the condition requires the missing attribute.","status":"open","priority":1,"issue_type":"bug","owner":"4678+seanb4t@users.noreply.github.com","created_at":"2026-03-10T00:02:06Z","created_by":"seanb4t","updated_at":"2026-03-10T00:02:06Z","external_ref":"https://github.com/holomush/holomush/pull/88"}

{"id":"holomush-phq3.28","title":"pkg/holo/emit.go: Character() stream prefix changed from char: to character: — persisted stream names break","description":"pkg/holo/emit.go line 21554: Emitter.Character() now writes events to stream 'character:\u003cid\u003e' instead of 'char:\u003cid\u003e'. This is a breaking change to the event stream naming contract in the public plugin SDK (pkg/ is the public API). Any subscriber that subscribed to 'char:\u003cid\u003e' streams will receive no future events; any replay of old events under the new prefix will miss historical data. The PR justifies this by saying 'no pre-release stores have been deployed' — but this claim is undocumented and unverifiable from the diff alone. If any operator has a staging or test PostgreSQL store with historical 'char:' prefixed events, those events will be orphaned. Furthermore, internal/core/event.go updates only the comment (line 10739-10740), not the actual data validation logic, so old events in the store with 'char:' prefix remain valid but invisible to new subscribers. The stream prefix is part of the public plugin SDK contract because Lua plugins use holo.emit.character() which routes through this path. No EventStore migration or compatibility layer is provided (the plan doc defers this to holomush-39vv). Location: pkg/holo/emit.go. Suggested fix: either provide a database migration renaming existing 'char:' streams or add a read-compatibility layer in the EventStore subscriber that accepts both prefixes during a transition window.","status":"open","priority":0,"issue_type":"bug","owner":"4678+seanb4t@users.noreply.github.com","created_at":"2026-03-10T00:13:33Z","created_by":"seanb4t","updated_at":"2026-03-10T00:13:33Z","external_ref":"https://github.com/holomush/holomush/pull/88"}

{"id":"holomush-phq3.41","title":"CharacterProvider uses bare string concatenation instead of access.CharacterSubject helper","description":"In internal/access/policy/attribute/character.go (line ~12848 in diff), the CharacterProvider builds a subject ID for role lookup using raw string concatenation:

    subjectID := "character:" + char.ID.String()

This PR explicitly introduces access.CharacterSubject() to eliminate exactly this pattern. The same file's package (internal/access/policy/attribute) already imports the access package for other uses, and CharacterProvider is the component responsible for resolving character attributes — making it the most likely place this helper would be used.

Replace with:
    subjectID := access.CharacterSubject(char.ID.String())

This is a one-liner fix but matters because the raw concatenation bypasses the empty-ID panic guard in CharacterSubject(), which is the safety net the helper is documented to provide.","status":"open","priority":2,"issue_type":"task","owner":"4678+seanb4t@users.noreply.github.com","created_at":"2026-03-10T00:16:32Z","created_by":"seanb4t","updated_at":"2026-03-10T00:16:32Z","external_ref":"https://github.com/holomush/holomush/pull/88"}

{"id":"holomush-phq3.39","title":"checkAccess: double-wrapping ErrPermissionDenied with redundant oops.Wrap","description":"In internal/world/service.go (line ~12819 in diff), the permission-denied path double-wraps ErrPermissionDenied:

    deniedErr := oops.With("reason", decision.Reason()).With("policy_id", decision.PolicyID()).Wrap(ErrPermissionDenied)
    return oops.Code(denyCode).Wrap(deniedErr)

The two-step wrap is unnecessary. oops.Code supports chaining, so this can be written as a single expression:

    return oops.Code(denyCode).
        With("reason", decision.Reason()).
        With("policy_id", decision.PolicyID()).
        Wrap(ErrPermissionDenied)

The intermediate variable `deniedErr` adds no value and obscures that both lines together form a single error construction. The evalutation-failure branch above it uses the single-expression style consistently, making this asymmetry a readability issue.","status":"open","priority":2,"issue_type":"task","owner":"4678+seanb4t@users.noreply.github.com","created_at":"2026-03-10T00:16:16Z","created_by":"seanb4t","updated_at":"2026-03-10T00:16:16Z","external_ref":"https://github.com/holomush/holomush/pull/88"}

{"id":"holomush-phq3.13","title":"safeResolve and safeResolveEnvironment log panic without context","description":"File: internal/access/policy/attribute/resolver.go

The panic-recovery deferred functions in safeResolve and safeResolveEnvironment call r.logger.Error(...) without the request context:

  r.logger.Error("provider panicked during resolution", "namespace", ..., "entity_id", ..., "panic", recovered)

All other error logging in this file was updated to use slog.ErrorContext(ctx, ...) or r.logger.ErrorContext(ctx, ...) to carry trace/span context. The panic path alone falls back to context-free logging, making it harder to correlate panics with the originating request in distributed tracing.

Suggested fix: pass ctx to the panic recovery log:
  r.logger.ErrorContext(ctx, "provider panicked during resolution", ...)","status":"open","priority":2,"issue_type":"task","owner":"4678+seanb4t@users.noreply.github.com","created_at":"2026-03-10T00:08:50Z","created_by":"seanb4t","updated_at":"2026-03-10T00:08:50Z","external_ref":"https://github.com/holomush/holomush/pull/88"}

{"id":"holomush-phq3.9","title":"who handler circuit breaker silently drops players from errorCount","description":"File: internal/command/handlers/who.go

When the circuit breaker trips (engineErrorCount >= maxEngineErrors), the loop breaks immediately. Sessions that are skipped by the break are never added to errorCount. As a result, the user-facing message 'N players could not be displayed due to system errors' understates the actual number of hidden players.

Example: 10 sessions, circuit breaks after the 3rd engine failure (at index 5). Sessions at indices 6-9 are silently dropped with no count. errorCount = 3, but 7 players total were not shown.

The circuit-breaker log entry does record 'skipped_sessions' for operator visibility, but the user-facing message is incorrect. This is a silent failure from the player's perspective.

Suggested fix: before breaking, add the remaining unqueried sessions to errorCount:
  remaining := len(sessions) - i - 1
  errorCount += remaining
  break","status":"open","priority":1,"issue_type":"bug","owner":"4678+seanb4t@users.noreply.github.com","created_at":"2026-03-10T00:08:31Z","created_by":"seanb4t","updated_at":"2026-03-10T00:08:31Z","external_ref":"https://github.com/holomush/holomush/pull/88"}

{"id":"holomush-phq3.45","title":"Fix(holomush-phq3.39): checkAccess double-wrapping","description":"Simplify checkAccess permission denied path in service.go:123-124 to single oops chain instead of double-wrapping","status":"open","priority":2,"issue_type":"task","owner":"4678+seanb4t@users.noreply.github.com","created_at":"2026-03-10T00:32:35Z","created_by":"seanb4t","updated_at":"2026-03-10T00:32:35Z"}

{"id":"holomush-phq3.38","title":"Plan acceptance criteria checkboxes use [x] semantically incorrectly for DEFERRED items","description":"The PR updates docs/plans/2026-02-06-full-abac-phase-7.6.md and marks several uncompleted acceptance criteria as '[x] ... [DEFERRED: holomush-xxx]'. In standard markdown task list convention, [x] means 'done/complete'.

Using [x] for deferred items creates a false impression that criteria are satisfied when they are actually moved to future work. This is particularly concerning for:
- '[x] AccessControl replaced with *policy.Engine in dependency graph [DEFERRED: holomush-ur6c]' — actually NOT done in this PR
- '[x] Security (S1): API ingress validation... [DEFERRED: holomush-dpsd]' — actually NOT done
- '[x] AttributeResolver wired... [DEFERRED: holomush-ur6c]' — actually NOT done

The plan document serves as an audit trail for the phased migration. Incorrectly marking items as [x] while deferring them undermines the ability to verify what has been completed vs. what is outstanding.

A more accurate approach would be to use a marker like '[-]' (skipped/deferred) or '[ ] [DEFERRED: ...]' to preserve the uncompleted status while documenting the tracking issue.

Files: docs/plans/2026-02-06-full-abac-phase-7.6.md (lines 1050-1055, 1058 in diff)

Suggested fix: Change [x] to [ ] for all DEFERRED items to accurately represent completion status. The DEFERRED notation alone (without the checkmark) makes the intent clear.","status":"open","priority":2,"issue_type":"task","owner":"4678+seanb4t@users.noreply.github.com","created_at":"2026-03-10T00:15:03Z","created_by":"seanb4t","updated_at":"2026-03-10T00:15:03Z","external_ref":"https://github.com/holomush/holomush/pull/88"}

{"id":"holomush-phq3.31","title":"docs/plans rollback section says 'Package 1-4' but plan defines 5 packages (T28-pkg1 through T28-pkg5)","description":"File: docs/plans/2026-02-06-full-abac-phase-7.6.md, rollback section

But the plan's own introduction states '**5 atomic commits** (T28-pkg1 through T28-pkg5)' and the acceptance criteria lists T28-pkg5 for the plugin package call site. The rollback text was not updated from the original 4-package plan.

{"id":"holomush-phq3.14","title":"AccessRequest fields are exported, allowing silent construction of zero-value requests","description":"File: internal/access/policy/types/types.go, lines ~89-98 (diff)

The PR adds NewAccessRequest() with empty-field validation, which is good. However, AccessRequest fields remain exported:

  type AccessRequest struct {
      Subject  string
      Action   string
      Resource string
  }

This means a caller can still bypass NewAccessRequest() entirely by using a struct literal:
  req := types.AccessRequest{Subject: "character:01ABC"}  // Action and Resource are empty strings

The engine's validateRequest() will catch this at evaluation time, but the error occurs far from the construction site, making it harder to diagnose. Additionally, code that uses struct literals directly misses the intent-signalling benefit of the constructor.

Suggested fix: Unexport the fields and remove the struct literal test (TestAccessRequest_Fields) which currently constructs directly. All callers would then be forced through NewAccessRequest(). The test file itself still uses struct literals in TestDecision_Validate cases (Decision{allowed: true, effect: ...}) within the same package, which is acceptable for same-package white-box tests.

Note: this is a suggestion rather than a bug — the engine catches the error, but the type expresses a weaker invariant than it could.","status":"open","priority":2,"issue_type":"task","owner":"4678+seanb4t@users.noreply.github.com","created_at":"2026-03-10T00:08:58Z","created_by":"seanb4t","updated_at":"2026-03-10T00:08:58Z","external_ref":"https://github.com/holomush/holomush/pull/88"}

{"id":"holomush-phq3.10","title":"Decision.SetPolicies/SetAttributes break encapsulation for incremental mutation","description":"File: internal/access/policy/types/types.go

The PR fully unexports Decision fields and adds accessor methods, which is the right move. However, it introduces two setter methods — SetPolicies() and SetAttributes() — to support incremental construction by the engine. This re-exposes mutation paths that the encapsulation was meant to close.

The comment in the source acknowledges this: 'A NewDecisionWithDetails constructor is not practical because the engine builds decisions incrementally.' However, the recommended path forward (consolidating packages) is deferred with 'if this design becomes a maintenance burden.'

The concrete problem: any caller with a *Decision pointer can call SetPolicies() or SetAttributes() after the fact, potentially in confusing order. The invariant between allowed/effect is protected by Validate(), but the policies and attributes fields can be mutated arbitrarily.

Suggested fix: Introduce a DecisionBuilder or a functional-options constructor so the Decision can be sealed after construction. For example:

  func NewDecisionWithDetails(effect Effect, reason, policyID string, opts ...DecisionOption) Decision

This avoids the need for setters entirely and keeps Decision immutable from the caller's perspective. The 'incremental build' concern is satisfied by building the Decision at the end of the evaluation pipeline with all details available.

Priority: important — the current code works correctly, but the setter pattern is a design debt the comment itself flags. A future reviewer may not notice that SetPolicies/SetAttributes were meant to be engine-internal-only.","status":"open","priority":1,"issue_type":"task","owner":"4678+seanb4t@users.noreply.github.com","created_at":"2026-03-10T00:08:32Z","created_by":"seanb4t","updated_at":"2026-03-10T00:08:32Z","external_ref":"https://github.com/holomush/holomush/pull/88"}

{"id":"holomush-phq3.11","title":"engine continues with partial attribute bags on resolver error, may produce incorrect policy decisions","description":"File: internal/access/policy/engine.go (Step 3, around line 101 in new diff)

Previously, when resolver.Resolve returned an error, the engine replaced bags with a fresh empty types.NewAttributeBags(). This was safe: no partial attribute data would influence the subsequent policy evaluation.

The new code keeps the partial bags returned by Resolve when there is a provider error. While this is documented as intentional ('bags may be partial when providers fail'), it introduces a correctness risk: an attribute-based policy condition that should be false (because the attribute wasn't loaded) could evaluate as true against stale/partial data from a previous or concurrent successful resolution — or evaluate against defaults the policy author did not intend.

Worse, Evaluate does not set any IsInfraFailure flag on the resulting decision to signal to callers that the evaluation was done with incomplete data. Callers only see the final Allow/Deny decision with no indication it came from partial attribute information.

The resolver error is logged at Warn level and execution continues silently to the full policy evaluation pipeline. If a permit policy's condition happens to match the partial bags, access is granted even though a required attribute was missing.

Suggested fix: When resolveErr != nil, either (1) return a deny Decision with IsInfraFailure=true immediately (fail-closed), or (2) at minimum wrap the returned decision with an IsInfraFailure indicator so callers can detect the degraded state.","status":"open","priority":1,"issue_type":"bug","owner":"4678+seanb4t@users.noreply.github.com","created_at":"2026-03-10T00:08:43Z","created_by":"seanb4t","updated_at":"2026-03-10T00:08:43Z","external_ref":"https://github.com/holomush/holomush/pull/88"}

{"id":"holomush-phq3.7","title":"migration_equivalence_test.go incorrectly comments builders do not have write:exit:* but test does not verify this","description":"In internal/access/migration_equivalence_test.go around line 379, the createCacheWithEquivalentPolicies function includes this comment:

  // Note: builders do NOT have write:exit:* in the static engine (see permissions.go builderPowers).

But the test cases include:
  {name: "builder - create exit", subject: ..., action: "write", resource: "exit:*"},
  {name: "builder - update exit", subject: ..., action: "write", resource: "exit:01STU"},
  {name: "builder - delete exit (denied)", subject: ..., action: "delete", resource: "exit:01STU"},

If builders truly do NOT have write:exit:*, then both "builder - create exit" and "builder - update exit" should produce 'denied' from the static engine. But no DSL policy granting builders exit:write is added to the equivalence policies, meaning both engines deny it.

If this accurately reflects the static engine (both deny), the test is correct but the test case names are misleading — "builder - create exit" implies a capability being tested, but actually both sides deny, which only confirms symmetric denial, not the interesting case.

More importantly: if the static engine DOES grant builders write:exit:*, but the policy cache doesn't include that policy, the equivalence test would show divergence. The comment acknowledges this with 'Note' but does not document it as an intentional divergence (the comment field in the test struct is empty).

Suggest either:
1. Add comment='intentional: builder cannot create/update exits' to the relevant test cases
2. Or verify and add the missing DSL exit policy if builders should have exit write access

File: internal/access/migration_equivalence_test.go","status":"open","priority":1,"issue_type":"bug","owner":"4678+seanb4t@users.noreply.github.com","created_at":"2026-03-10T00:04:31Z","created_by":"seanb4t","updated_at":"2026-03-10T00:04:31Z","external_ref":"https://github.com/holomush/holomush/pull/88"}

{"id":"holomush-phq3.22","title":"holomush.list_commands() Lua API return format changed without versioning or migration guide","description":"The Lua host function holomush.list_commands() changed its first return value from a flat array table (commands table, error string) to a structured wrapper table ({commands: [...], incomplete: bool}, error string). This is a breaking change for any Lua plugin that calls list_commands() and iterates the result directly. The in-tree help plugin (plugins/help/main.lua) is updated, but external plugins written by game operators must update their code. The PR summary acknowledges this but there is no developer-facing migration guide (e.g., in site/docs/developers/). The file internal/plugin/hostfunc/commands.go line 1302 changes the return contract. Additionally, the error handling behavior changed: previously an error caused (nil, error_string) and the plugin checked err first; now the function can return a non-nil result AND a non-nil error simultaneously (when hadEngineError=true), which is a subtle protocol change that operator-written plugins may not handle correctly. Location: internal/plugin/hostfunc/commands.go. Suggested fix: add a migration note to site/docs/developers/ documenting the new return shape and the nil-result-vs-wrapped-result distinction before merging.","status":"open","priority":1,"issue_type":"bug","owner":"4678+seanb4t@users.noreply.github.com","created_at":"2026-03-10T00:12:53Z","created_by":"seanb4t","updated_at":"2026-03-10T00:12:53Z","external_ref":"https://github.com/holomush/holomush/pull/88"}

{"id":"holomush-phq3.37","title":"Lua API return format change (structured result with 'incomplete' flag) not documented in spec or ADR","description":"The PR changes the Lua host function listCommandsFn return format from a plain commands table to a structured result object:

OLD: returns (commands_table, nil_or_error_string) — table of command entries
NEW: returns ({commands: [...], incomplete: bool}, nil_or_error_string) — result struct

This is a breaking API change to the plugin-facing Lua SDK. The help plugin (plugins/help/main.lua) is updated in this PR to use result.commands, but any third-party plugin relying on the old return format (direct table indexing) would break silently since Lua tables just return nil for unknown keys.

The plan (docs/plans/2026-02-06-full-abac-phase-7.6.md) does not mention this API change. There is no ADR for it. The CLAUDE.md spec-driven development rule states 'Work MUST NOT start without a spec/design/plan' and architectural decisions require ADRs.

The change is semantically justified (infra failures need the 'incomplete' flag), but it lacks:
1. A spec reference or decision document for the breaking Lua API change
2. Documentation in pkg/holo/ or site/docs/developers/ about the new return format
3. Backward compatibility consideration (nil check on result.incomplete for old plugins)

Files: internal/plugin/hostfunc/commands.go (lines 11302-11378 in diff), plugins/help/main.lua (line 21604)

Suggested fix: Either add an ADR documenting this breaking plugin API change, or add a note in the plan that this API change is part of Phase 7.6 scope. Also update developer documentation for plugin authors.","status":"open","priority":2,"issue_type":"task","owner":"4678+seanb4t@users.noreply.github.com","created_at":"2026-03-10T00:14:52Z","created_by":"seanb4t","updated_at":"2026-03-10T00:14:52Z","external_ref":"https://github.com/holomush/holomush/pull/88"}

{"id":"holomush-phq3.2","title":"list_commands Lua API returns incomplete results without clear contract for callers","description":"File: internal/plugin/hostfunc/commands.go (listCommandsFn / canExecuteCommand)

When engine errors occur during command capability filtering, listCommandsFn returns a result table with incomplete=true plus a non-nil error string as the second return value. Lua callers that pattern-match on `if err then` will see the error correctly, but callers written as `commands, _ = holomush.list_commands(id)` (discarding the error) will silently receive a partial command list.

The security concern is subtle: if a privileged command's capability check fails (engine error), the command will be excluded from the returned list (fail-closed on the capability check itself is correct), but the caller has no reliable way to know the list is incomplete unless it also checks the error string. A plugin that uses list_commands to build a security-sensitive UI or access menu could display fewer options than actually exist, potentially confusing users or operators about what is permitted.

Additionally, the incomplete flag is only set on the result table, not in the error return. Lua callers may need to check both.

Suggested fix: Document in the Lua plugin API that the error return from list_commands MUST always be checked, and add a note that incomplete=true means capability filtering was partial. Consider adding a contract test with the help plugin that verifies incomplete results are surfaced to users.","status":"open","priority":2,"issue_type":"task","owner":"4678+seanb4t@users.noreply.github.com","created_at":"2026-03-10T00:02:17Z","created_by":"seanb4t","updated_at":"2026-03-10T00:02:17Z","external_ref":"https://github.com/holomush/holomush/pull/88"}

{"id":"holomush-phq3.18","title":"GetCharactersByLocation action change from 'read' to 'list_characters' has no migration equivalence test","description":"The PR changes GetCharactersByLocation at internal/world/service.go:1041-1046 to use action='list_characters' (decomposed from legacy compound resource 'location:<id>:characters'). This is a semantic behavior change per ADR #76, but:

1. service_test.go has no dedicated test for GetCharactersByLocation with the new action (the test coverage focuses on Location/Exit/Object/Character entity types individually).
2. TestMigrationEquivalence in internal/access/migration_equivalence_test.go explicitly scopes itself to admin, builder, and player roles for read/write/delete/execute actions — it does NOT include any test case for 'list_characters' action, which means the equivalence between StaticAccessControl.Check(ctx, subject, 'read', 'location:<id>:characters') and AccessPolicyEngine.Evaluate(ctx, {action='list_characters', resource='location:<id>'}) is never validated.

The comment in the code notes this was intentional ('Compound Resource Decomposition per ADR #76') but that makes equivalence testing MORE critical, not less — the policy seed data must grant 'list_characters' on location resources for player/builder/admin roles, and there is no test verifying this grant exists.

Risk: If the seed policies don't have a 'list_characters' grant, the 'who' command and any command displaying characters at a location would silently deny access for all users after migration, with no test catching it.

Suggested fix: Add a test case to TestMigrationEquivalence covering:
  {name: 'player - list characters at location', subject: 'character:player-01DEF', action: 'list_characters', resource: 'location:01JKL'}
And verify the grant exists in the policy seed.","status":"open","priority":1,"issue_type":"bug","owner":"4678+seanb4t@users.noreply.github.com","created_at":"2026-03-10T00:10:25Z","created_by":"seanb4t","updated_at":"2026-03-10T00:10:25Z","external_ref":"https://github.com/holomush/holomush/pull/88"}

{"id":"holomush-phq3.34","title":"Security S8 CI check not implemented: static analysis for remaining AccessControl.Check() calls","description":"The plan's acceptance criteria requires (S8 - holomush-5k1.355): 'A static analysis rule or go vet check MUST verify no remaining AccessControl.Check() calls post-migration. CI MUST enforce this check.'

The PR adds a lint:access-migration task in Taskfile.yaml (line ~864-908 of diff) that checks for 'AccessControl.Check(ctx,' patterns and is added to CI via .github/workflows/ci.yaml. This addresses part of S8.

However, the acceptance criterion remains unchecked in the plan update (line 1059 of diff still shows '- [ ] Security (S8...)'. This is an inconsistency: the implementation appears to be present (the lint task was added) but the checkbox was not checked.

More importantly, the lint pattern 'AccessControl\.Check\(ctx,' only catches the specific 4-argument form. It would NOT catch:
- capability.Enforcer.Check() calls (acknowledged in the comment, but no corresponding check ensures capability.Enforcer.Check is also banned post-migration)
- future regressions where someone adds a new AccessControl interface with a different Check signature

The plan spec (Task 29) requires CI check to be 'MUST enforce' — the task is present and wired to CI, so this criterion appears substantially met, but the plan checkbox was not updated.

Files: docs/plans/2026-02-06-full-abac-phase-7.6.md (line 1059), Taskfile.yaml (lines 864-888 in diff), .github/workflows/ci.yaml (lines 765-767 in diff)

Suggested fix: Mark S8 as [x] in the plan since the lint:access-migration task implements it. Optionally expand the check to also verify capability.Enforcer.Check() is absent post-Task-29.","status":"open","priority":1,"issue_type":"bug","owner":"4678+seanb4t@users.noreply.github.com","created_at":"2026-03-10T00:14:16Z","created_by":"seanb4t","updated_at":"2026-03-10T00:14:16Z","external_ref":"https://github.com/holomush/holomush/pull/88"}

{"id":"holomush-phq3.33","title":"Security S1 (API ingress system-subject bypass) marked DEFERRED without tracking status","description":"The plan's acceptance criterion Security (S1) states: 'API ingress validation added to prevent external requests from using system subject or WithSystemSubject(ctx) bypass mechanism.' This is a hard security requirement checked with 'MUST' semantics in the plan.

In the PR diff, this is marked: '[x] Security (S1): API ingress validation added... [DEFERRED: holomush-dpsd]'

The checkbox marker [x] with DEFERRED is semantically contradictory — marking it checked implies it is done, but the note says it is deferred to another bead. The actual implementation is absent from the diff: no API ingress middleware was added, no WithSystemSubject guard was added at the gRPC or WebSocket boundary.

This is a security regression: the new AccessPolicyEngine accepts a 'system' subject that bypasses all policy evaluation (per the engine design). Without ingress validation, a crafted external request could inject the system subject and bypass ABAC. The old StaticAccessControl did not have this bypass surface.

The spec (docs/plans/2026-02-06-full-abac-phase-7.6.md, acceptance criteria) lists S1 as a Task 28 requirement, not a future phase requirement.

Files: docs/plans/2026-02-06-full-abac-phase-7.6.md (line 1058 in diff), no corresponding implementation file added.

Suggested fix: Either add the ingress validation in this PR, or revert the [x] to [ ] and add a DEFERRED note without checking the box (to avoid false impression of compliance), and ensure holomush-dpsd is a blocking dependency of this PR's merge.","status":"open","priority":0,"issue_type":"bug","owner":"4678+seanb4t@users.noreply.github.com","created_at":"2026-03-10T00:14:02Z","created_by":"seanb4t","updated_at":"2026-03-10T00:14:02Z","external_ref":"https://github.com/holomush/holomush/pull/88"}

{"id":"holomush-phq3.32","title":"DI wiring and full production wiring marked [DEFERRED] without engine being nil-safe at runtime","description":"The plan's acceptance criteria for DI wiring include: AccessControl replaced with *policy.Engine in dependency graph, AttributeResolver wired, PolicyCache wired and Listen() called, SessionResolver wired, AuditLogger wired. All six are marked [x] DEFERRED to holomush-ur6c.

The plan spec (docs/plans/2026-02-06-full-abac-phase-7.6.md) states these are acceptance criteria for Task 28 — they are not optional deferral items. Deferring them means the production binary is running with call sites that call engine.Evaluate() through the new checkAccess() helper while the engine is not production-wired (no real AttributeResolver, SessionResolver, AuditLogger, or PolicyCache.Listen()). The tests pass because unit tests use policytest.NewGrantEngine() or policytest.NewErrorEngine().

This means in a real deployment: (1) the AccessPolicyEngine used in production is either nil (causing panic) or the lightweight policy.Engine without proper attribute resolution, (2) decisions will be based on empty attribute bags (no character roles, no location context), causing silent denials for all players, and (3) audit logging does not happen.

The spec section (07-migration-seeds.md) is explicit: 'All call sites switch to Evaluate() directly.' This requires the full engine stack to be operational, not just the interface wired.

The commit message 'feat(access): ABAC Phase 7.6 — Migrate to AccessPolicyEngine' implies the migration is complete, but the engine is not production-wired.

Files: docs/plans/2026-02-06-full-abac-phase-7.6.md (lines 1050-1055 in diff), cmd/holomush/ (no change to engine wiring in diff)

Suggested fix: Either (a) block merging until holomush-ur6c is resolved and the engine is production-wired, or (b) document clearly in the PR that this is a partial migration and the production binary still uses a stub/nil engine, and add a nil-guard in checkAccess() that fails-closed with an explicit error rather than panicking.","status":"open","priority":0,"issue_type":"bug","owner":"4678+seanb4t@users.noreply.github.com","created_at":"2026-03-10T00:13:50Z","created_by":"seanb4t","updated_at":"2026-03-10T00:13:50Z","external_ref":"https://github.com/holomush/holomush/pull/88"}

{"id":"holomush-e41x.12","title":"S1 ingress guard returns non-zero Decision alongside non-nil error, violating interface contract","description":"internal/access/policy/engine.go line 64-65: the S1 ingress guard returns a non-zero Decision (EffectDeny, reason='system subject requires system context', policyID='ingress_guard') alongside a non-nil error.

The AccessPolicyEngine interface contract in types/types.go states: 'The engine never returns a permissive Decision alongside an error; on error paths the returned Decision is always the zero value (EffectDefaultDeny, allowed=false).'

EffectDeny != EffectDefaultDeny. The contract specifies the zero value (which is EffectDefaultDeny), but the S1 path returns EffectDeny. This means callers that inspect decision.Effect() alongside a non-nil error will see an effect that contradicts the documented contract.

Current callers all short-circuit on non-nil error before inspecting the decision, so no incorrect access grants result today. But the contract violation is a maintenance hazard: a future caller following the interface docs could incorrectly assume the decision is zero-valued on error paths.

Fix: change line 64 to return types.Decision{} (zero value) instead of the constructed Deny decision. The error return already carries all the classification information needed.

{"id":"holomush-e41x.1","title":"Policy cache stale misclassified as ErrPermissionDenied instead of ErrAccessEvaluationFailed","description":"In internal/access/policy/engine.go, the stale-cache denial is created with:

    types.NewDecision(types.EffectDefaultDeny, "policy cache stale", "")

The policyID is empty (""), so Decision.IsInfraFailure() (which checks strings.HasPrefix(policyID, "infra:")) returns false.

As a result, when world/service.go checkAccess evaluates this decision:
  - decision.IsInfraFailure() == false
  - It falls through to the ErrPermissionDenied branch

This misclassifies an infrastructure failure (stale policy cache) as a policy denial. Consequences:
1. Users receive "You don't have permission to do that" instead of "Permission check failed. Please try again."
2. observability.RecordEngineFailure is not called — the stale-cache condition is invisible in metrics.
3. Operators have no way to alert on stale caches from access denial metrics alone.

Fix: Use policyID "infra:policy-cache-stale" so IsInfraFailure() returns true, consistent with other infra denial paths (infra:session-invalid, infra:session-store-error).

Location: internal/access/policy/engine.go, Step 3b block (around line 118 in the new file).","status":"open","priority":1,"issue_type":"bug","owner":"4678+seanb4t@users.noreply.github.com","created_at":"2026-03-10T12:44:37Z","created_by":"seanb4t","updated_at":"2026-03-10T12:44:37Z","external_ref":"https://github.com/holomush/holomush/pull/88"}

{"id":"holomush-e41x.14","title":"Fix(holomush-e41x.5): rename cache_test_helpers.go to test-only file","description":"Rename cache_test_helpers.go to prevent production imports of test helper","status":"open","priority":2,"issue_type":"task","owner":"4678+seanb4t@users.noreply.github.com","created_at":"2026-03-10T12:59:06Z","created_by":"seanb4t","updated_at":"2026-03-10T12:59:06Z"}

{"id":"holomush-e41x.2","title":"Partial attribute bag evaluation produces incorrect decisions without surfacing to callers","description":"In internal/access/policy/engine.go Step 3 (attribute resolution), when providers fail:

    bags, resolveErr := e.resolver.Resolve(ctx, req)
    if resolveErr != nil {
        slog.WarnContext(...)  // logged but evaluation continues
    }

The engine proceeds to evaluate policies against an incomplete attribute bag. This is documented as intentional (avoids total outage on provider failure), but has two unaddressed problems:

1. The decision returned to callers does NOT signal that it was made on partial data. IsInfraFailure() returns false, and there is no "partial_data" flag or policyID prefix, so checkAccess (world/service.go) cannot distinguish a reliable deny from a deny-due-to-missing-attributes. An attribute-dependent permit policy could incorrectly deny (safe, fail-closed) OR a policy that should have been a deny could accidentally permit if the required deny-condition attributes are the ones that failed to load (unsafe).

2. The observability counter RecordEngineFailure is not called on the partial-bag path, only slog.WarnContext. This means attribute resolution failures are invisible to the metrics/alerting layer.

Suggested fix for (2): call observability.RecordEngineFailure("attribute_resolution") when resolveErr != nil.
For (1): consider a "infra:partial-attributes" policyID on the stale path and documenting which policies are safe vs. unsafe under partial bags.

Location: internal/access/policy/engine.go, Step 3 (around line 130 in new file).","status":"open","priority":1,"issue_type":"bug","owner":"4678+seanb4t@users.noreply.github.com","created_at":"2026-03-10T12:44:50Z","created_by":"seanb4t","updated_at":"2026-03-10T12:44:50Z","external_ref":"https://github.com/holomush/holomush/pull/88"}

{"id":"holomush-e41x.7","title":"Attribute resolver silently proceeds with partial bags on provider failure — incorrect allow decisions possible","description":"In internal/access/policy/engine.go (Step 3), when attribute resolution partially fails (resolveErr != nil), the engine logs a warning and proceeds with whatever attribute bags were populated. The comment explicitly acknowledges this: 'This means policies evaluate against incomplete data on provider errors, which may cause incorrect allow/deny decisions.'

This is a deliberate design decision documented in the comment, but the consequence is a security-relevant one: if the role attribute fails to resolve (e.g., DB timeout fetching character), a policy that checks role='admin' to deny access could incorrectly allow a request because the role attribute is missing and the policy condition evaluates as not-matched (falling to default-deny). Conversely, a policy that checks role='player' to allow access could deny a player due to missing role.

This is a valid design tradeoff (availability over strict security), but it should be tracked because:
1. The engine interface doc (types.go) says 'Callers MUST treat any non-nil error from Evaluate as a denial' — but partial bag errors are NOT surfaced as engine errors, they are swallowed (only logged).
2. The interface contract and the engine implementation are inconsistent: partial bag failure produces a non-error return with a potentially wrong decision.

Suggestion: either surface resolveErr as the error return (making the engine fail-closed on provider errors), or add an explicit IsPartial() bool on Decision so callers can detect and handle degraded evaluation.","status":"open","priority":1,"issue_type":"bug","owner":"4678+seanb4t@users.noreply.github.com","created_at":"2026-03-10T12:45:49Z","created_by":"seanb4t","updated_at":"2026-03-10T12:45:49Z","external_ref":"https://github.com/holomush/holomush/pull/88"}

{"id":"holomush-e41x.9","title":"Migration equivalence test excludes player role location-based permissions","description":"The TestMigrationEquivalence test in internal/access/migration_equivalence_test.go explicitly acknowledges it cannot test player role location-based permissions ($here token) because the static engine requires a LocationResolver. The test comment says: 'Location-based permission equivalence is validated separately in production integration tests where LocationResolver is available.'

The integration test that partially fills this gap is test/integration/access/location_equivalence_test.go, which covers read:location and emit:stream:location for the current location. However, the integration test does NOT cover:
1. write:character:<id> (move character) — covered by static engine's player $self permission
2. read:character:<other-at-location> — the $here co-location read case
3. Location-based self-read equivalence (character:player reading character:player via $self)
4. The migrate test explicitly excludes 'player - execute basic command' due to $here-expansion

This gap is important because the player role has the most complex permission logic ($here, $self tokens), and this is exactly the area where migration divergence is most likely. A regression in player-role $here resolution could go undetected by the unit equivalence test.

Location: internal/access/migration_equivalence_test.go (lines 210-230 — the LIMITATION comment), test/integration/access/location_equivalence_test.go.

Suggested fix: Extend test/integration/access/location_equivalence_test.go (or add a new test) to cover:
- write:character:<charID> equivalence (player moving self via $self)
- read:character:<colocated-other> equivalence via co-location attribute","status":"open","priority":1,"issue_type":"task","owner":"4678+seanb4t@users.noreply.github.com","created_at":"2026-03-10T12:46:09Z","created_by":"seanb4t","updated_at":"2026-03-10T12:46:09Z","external_ref":"https://github.com/holomush/holomush/pull/88"}

{"id":"holomush-e41x.11","title":"policytest helpers lack tests for their own correctness contracts","description":"The new internal/access/policy/policytest helpers (AllowAllEngine, DenyAllEngine, GrantEngine, ErrorEngine, InfraFailureEngine) are production test infrastructure used across 50+ tests in the PR, but the helpers themselves have no dedicated tests validating their contract.

Specific concerns:
1. GrantEngine.Evaluate returns EffectDefaultDeny (not EffectDeny) for ungranted permissions — callers may treat these differently if they check decision.Effect() directly rather than IsAllowed(). The helpers.go file shows: 'return types.NewDecision(types.EffectDefaultDeny, "test-no-grant", ""), nil'. If any production code distinguishes EffectDeny vs EffectDefaultDeny (which CheckCapability does), GrantEngine may produce misleading test results.
2. ErrorEngine returns types.Decision{} (zero-value) plus an error — zero-value Decision has EffectDefaultDeny by invariant (confirmed by TestDecision_ZeroValue_DeniesAccess), but this is only correct if callers check the error first.
3. InfraFailureEngine.policyID field is used to trigger IsInfraFailure() prefix check — a typo in test setup (e.g., 'infra-session' instead of 'infra:session') would silently produce wrong behavior.

These are testing utilities only, so the risk is that subtle bugs in the helpers produce false green tests without failing test output.

Location: internal/access/policy/policytest/helpers.go

Suggested fix: Add a TestPolicyTestHelpers table test validating that AllowAllEngine returns IsAllowed()==true, DenyAllEngine returns IsAllowed()==false with EffectDeny, GrantEngine returns DefaultDeny (not Deny) for ungranted requests, and InfraFailureEngine produces IsInfraFailure()==true decisions.","status":"open","priority":2,"issue_type":"task","owner":"4678+seanb4t@users.noreply.github.com","created_at":"2026-03-10T12:46:24Z","created_by":"seanb4t","updated_at":"2026-03-10T12:46:24Z","external_ref":"https://github.com/holomush/holomush/pull/88"}

{"id":"holomush-gk8b.13","title":"subscriber.go context fix and codes.go fmt.Fprintf are unrelated to ABAC migration scope","description":"Files:
- internal/plugin/subscriber.go (context leak fix)
- pkg/holo/codes.go (fmt.Fprintf optimization)

These two changes are unrelated to the ABAC Phase 7.6 migration:

1. subscriber.go: Fixes a context leak where context.WithTimeout was called outside the goroutine. This is a legitimate bug fix but should have been a separate commit/PR for clean git history.

2. codes.go: Changes result.WriteString(fmt.Sprintf(...)) to fmt.Fprintf(&result, ...). This is a minor performance optimization unrelated to access control.

Per YAGNI and the project's convention of atomic commits, these should ideally be separate changes. The subscriber.go fix is a real bug fix that deserves its own commit message describing the issue.

This is a minor process concern, not blocking.","status":"open","priority":2,"issue_type":"task","owner":"4678+seanb4t@users.noreply.github.com","created_at":"2026-03-10T14:24:42Z","created_by":"seanb4t","updated_at":"2026-03-10T14:24:42Z","external_ref":"https://github.com/holomush/holomush/pull/88"}

{"id":"holomush-gk8b.1","title":"SetHandler does not guard ErrAccessEvaluationFailed before writeOutputWithWorldError (double-log + code loss)","description":"File: internal/command/handlers/objects.go, lines 170-177

SetHandler's applyProperty error path does not check errors.Is(err, world.ErrAccessEvaluationFailed) before calling slog.ErrorContext + writeOutputWithWorldError. Every other command handler (look, move, createObject, createLocation) guards this sentinel and returns the error as-is to preserve the specific oops code (e.g. LOCATION_ACCESS_EVALUATION_FAILED).

When applyProperty fails due to an access evaluation failure:
1. SetHandler logs it again at slog.ErrorContext (double-log — world.checkAccess already logged it)
2. writeOutputWithWorldError re-wraps it as CodeWorldError, losing the entity-specific evaluation-failure code
3. The user gets 'Failed to set property. Please try again.' instead of the engine-failure message that lets the command framework distinguish infra failure from policy denial

Fix: add the same guard that other handlers use:

    if errors.Is(err, world.ErrAccessEvaluationFailed) {
        return err
    }

before the slog.ErrorContext + writeOutputWithWorldError call at line 170-177.","status":"open","priority":1,"issue_type":"bug","owner":"4678+seanb4t@users.noreply.github.com","created_at":"2026-03-10T14:21:57Z","created_by":"seanb4t","updated_at":"2026-03-10T14:21:57Z","external_ref":"https://github.com/holomush/holomush/pull/88"}

{"id":"holomush-gk8b.14","title":"Fix(holomush-gk8b.1): add ErrAccessEvaluationFailed guard in SetHandler","description":"Add errors.Is(err, world.ErrAccessEvaluationFailed) guard before slog.ErrorContext + writeOutputWithWorldError in SetHandler, matching pattern from look/move/createObject handlers.","status":"open","priority":2,"issue_type":"task","owner":"4678+seanb4t@users.noreply.github.com","created_at":"2026-03-10T14:32:16Z","created_by":"seanb4t","updated_at":"2026-03-10T14:32:16Z"}

{"id":"holomush-gk8b.8","title":"migration equivalence test gated behind integration build tag — not run in standard CI","description":"TestMigrationEquivalence in internal/access/migration_equivalence_test.go has the build tag //go:build integration. This test is the primary regression guard for the entire Phase 7.6 migration — it checks that StaticAccessControl.Check() and AccessPolicyEngine.Evaluate() produce identical decisions for all 29 production call sites.

Because the test is behind the integration tag it does NOT run in the standard 'task test' pipeline; it only runs via 'go test -tags=integration'. This means a future PR that alters policy seeding or role definitions would not be caught by the default CI gate.

The plan (docs/plans/2026-02-06-full-abac-phase-7.6.md) calls this equivalence test 'critical for Phase 7.6 migration', but its build tag means any break in equivalence would be invisible to standard 'task test'.

Additionally, the test explicitly documents a LIMITATION: player-role location-based permissions (using the $here token with LocationResolver) are excluded from the equivalence check. There is no companion integration test that validates this remaining gap.

Recommendation: either run migration_equivalence_test.go without the integration tag (it uses only in-memory fixtures), or add it to a required CI step that runs before merge.","status":"open","priority":1,"issue_type":"task","owner":"4678+seanb4t@users.noreply.github.com","created_at":"2026-03-10T14:22:58Z","created_by":"seanb4t","updated_at":"2026-03-10T14:22:58Z","external_ref":"https://github.com/holomush/holomush/pull/88"}

{"id":"holomush-gk8b.16","title":"Fix(holomush-gk8b.8): remove integration build tag from migration equivalence test","description":"Remove //go:build integration tag from migration_equivalence_test.go so it runs in standard task test pipeline. Test uses only in-memory fixtures.","status":"open","priority":2,"issue_type":"task","owner":"4678+seanb4t@users.noreply.github.com","created_at":"2026-03-10T14:32:19Z","created_by":"seanb4t","updated_at":"2026-03-10T14:32:19Z"}

{"id":"holomush-gk8b.3","title":"propertyQuerier/propertyMutator silently converts ErrPermissionDenied into CodeWorldError","description":"File: internal/command/handlers/objects.go, lines 270-337 (propertyQuerier.GetLocation, GetObject, propertyMutator.UpdateLocation, UpdateObject)

These helpers guard only ErrAccessEvaluationFailed and re-wrap everything else (including ErrPermissionDenied) as CodeWorldError. When the set command is denied by policy, the player receives 'Failed to set property. Please try again.' instead of a permission-denied message.

This is inconsistent with all other command handlers (look, move, createObject, createLocation) which preserve ErrPermissionDenied and allow the command framework to surface the correct user message.

Additionally, ErrPermissionDenied is also buried by the outer SetHandler which re-wraps the result of applyProperty as WorldError without checking for ErrPermissionDenied either. This means neither the user nor the error code in the response correctly reflects that the failure was a permission denial.

Fix: add a parallel check for world.ErrPermissionDenied in each propertyQuerier/propertyMutator helper, returning it as-is (same pattern as ErrAccessEvaluationFailed), so the set command can distinguish denial from infra failure.","status":"open","priority":1,"issue_type":"bug","owner":"4678+seanb4t@users.noreply.github.com","created_at":"2026-03-10T14:22:13Z","created_by":"seanb4t","updated_at":"2026-03-10T14:22:13Z","external_ref":"https://github.com/holomush/holomush/pull/88"}

{"id":"holomush-gk8b.2","title":"applyProperty wraps errors with fmt.Errorf instead of oops, losing structured error context","description":"File: internal/command/handlers/objects.go, line 260

applyProperty uses fmt.Errorf("set property: %w", err) to wrap errors returned from definition.Set(). This is the only place in the changed files using fmt.Errorf for error wrapping — all other callsites use oops.

The fmt.Errorf preserves errors.Is/errors.As chains, so the sentinel check in SetHandler works, but it loses all oops structured context (code, with-fields) that would appear in observability tooling.

Fix: use oops.Wrapf or oops.Code(...).With(...).Wrapf to preserve the structured context, consistent with the rest of the codebase.","status":"open","priority":2,"issue_type":"task","owner":"4678+seanb4t@users.noreply.github.com","created_at":"2026-03-10T14:22:04Z","created_by":"seanb4t","updated_at":"2026-03-10T14:22:04Z","external_ref":"https://github.com/holomush/holomush/pull/88"}

{"id":"holomush-gk8b.7","title":"G706 (log injection) lint suppression is overly broad","description":"File: .golangci.yaml (lines added in this PR)

The G706 suppression uses path regex `(internal|cmd|pkg)/.+\.go$` which covers ALL production Go files in those directories. The comment says 'Scoped to known slog-only packages' but the regex is a catch-all that matches any .go file under internal/, cmd/, or pkg/.

If any future code in these directories uses fmt.Sprintf-based logging or direct string concatenation in log messages (not slog structured logging), the G706 rule would correctly flag it but the suppression would silence the warning.

Suggested fix: Narrow the path regex to specific packages that are known to use only slog, or use a per-file nolint directive. Example:
  path: 'internal/(access|command|world|plugin|observability)/.+\.go$'

This way, new packages added under internal/ are still checked by default until explicitly exempted.","status":"open","priority":1,"issue_type":"task","owner":"4678+seanb4t@users.noreply.github.com","created_at":"2026-03-10T14:22:56Z","created_by":"seanb4t","updated_at":"2026-03-10T14:22:56Z","external_ref":"https://github.com/holomush/holomush/pull/88"}

{"id":"holomush-gk8b.10","title":"no test for GetCharactersByLocation returning ErrAccessEvaluationFailed on infra failure","description":"TestWorldService_GetCharactersByLocation_UsesDecomposedResource and TestWorldService_GetCharactersByLocation_VerifiesAccessRequest correctly verify the ADR #76 compound resource decomposition. However, neither test nor any other GetCharactersByLocation test covers the infra-failure decision path (engine returns nil error but decision.IsInfraFailure() == true).

The checkAccess helper is unit tested exhaustively for the infra-failure case in service_helpers_test.go, including CHARACTER and EXIT prefixes. The GetCharactersByLocation call uses entityPrefix='CHARACTER', so the infra-failure path is reachable. The gap is that there is no test verifying GetCharactersByLocation returns ErrAccessEvaluationFailed (not ErrPermissionDenied) when the engine signals an infra failure with action='list_characters'.

This is consistent with how AddSceneParticipant, RemoveSceneParticipant, and GetLocation all have explicit infra-failure test cases in service_test.go. GetCharactersByLocation is the only heavily-refactored method missing this case.

File: internal/world/service_test.go, TestWorldService_GetCharactersByLocation","status":"open","priority":2,"issue_type":"task","owner":"4678+seanb4t@users.noreply.github.com","created_at":"2026-03-10T14:23:08Z","created_by":"seanb4t","updated_at":"2026-03-10T14:23:08Z","external_ref":"https://github.com/holomush/holomush/pull/88"}

{"id":"holomush-gk8b.12","title":"list_commands circuit breaker threshold behavior not tested in hostfunc commands_test.go","description":"The listCommandsFn circuit breaker (internal/plugin/hostfunc/commands.go) breaks after maxEngineErrors=3 consecutive engine failures, then skips remaining commands. This is an important behavioral contract: commands that appear after the 3rd failure in the iteration order are dropped without being evaluated, and hadEngineError is set to true.

The test TestListCommands_EngineError_HidesCapabilityCommands only tests with a single capability command (boot), which means the circuit breaker never has a chance to trip mid-list (it trips on the first failure, but there are no more commands to skip). There is no test that:
1. Registers 5+ commands each with one capability
2. Uses an engine that errors on every call
3. Verifies that the circuit trips after exactly 3 failures
4. Verifies that commands after position 3 are also hidden

This matters because the circuit breaker carries a behavioral assumption about command ordering. If the threshold or the break logic were changed (e.g., to maxEngineErrors=1 or the break removed entirely), the existing tests would not catch the regression.

File: internal/plugin/hostfunc/commands.go lines 1644-1660, commands_test.go","status":"open","priority":1,"issue_type":"task","owner":"4678+seanb4t@users.noreply.github.com","created_at":"2026-03-10T14:23:21Z","created_by":"seanb4t","updated_at":"2026-03-10T14:23:21Z","external_ref":"https://github.com/holomush/holomush/pull/88"}

{"id":"holomush-gk8b.19","title":"objects.go handler ErrAccessEvaluationFailed passthrough paths are untested","description":"The PR adds errors.Is(err, world.ErrAccessEvaluationFailed) passthrough guards in four locations in internal/command/handlers/objects.go:
- createObject (line 87): returns engine-error as-is instead of wrapping as WORLD_ERROR
- createLocation (line 100): same pattern
- SetHandler -> applyProperty (line 113-114): passes through ErrAccessEvaluationFailed and ErrPermissionDenied
- propertyQuerier.GetLocation (line 145-150): same two guards

The corresponding test file (objects_test.go) has no test cases exercising these new branches. The existing tests (TestCreateHandler_ObjectServiceError, TestCreateHandler_LocationServiceError, TestSetHandler_UpdateLocationFailure, etc.) only cover DB-level failures after a successful access check.

Without tests, the following scenarios are unverified:
1. When CreateObject/CreateLocation returns ErrAccessEvaluationFailed, the handler passes the original oops code through (not masked as WORLD_ERROR)
2. When SetHandler's applyProperty returns ErrAccessEvaluationFailed, the handler propagates correctly
3. The player-visible message for these failures is the 'Permission check failed. Please try again' message rather than 'System error'

Suggested fix: Add test cases to objects_test.go using a world.Service backed by policytest.NewErrorEngine() and assert the returned error code is ACCESS_EVALUATION_FAILED (not WORLD_ERROR). Pattern established by TestMoveHandler_AccessEvaluationFailureOnGetExits in move_test.go.","status":"open","priority":2,"issue_type":"task","owner":"4678+seanb4t@users.noreply.github.com","created_at":"2026-03-10T16:13:26Z","created_by":"seanb4t","updated_at":"2026-03-10T16:13:26Z","external_ref":"https://github.com/holomush/holomush/pull/88"}

{"id":"holomush-gk8b.28","title":"Fix(holomush-gk8b.19): add objects handler engine-error tests","description":"Add test cases for ErrAccessEvaluationFailed passthrough in objects.go handlers","status":"open","priority":2,"issue_type":"task","owner":"4678+seanb4t@users.noreply.github.com","created_at":"2026-03-10T16:32:24Z","created_by":"seanb4t","updated_at":"2026-03-10T16:32:24Z"}

{"id":"holomush-gk8b.17","title":"ErrCapabilityCheckFailed not in error chain for engine-level errors in CheckCapability","description":"In internal/command/access.go, CheckCapability documents that callers can use errors.Is(err, ErrCapabilityCheckFailed) to distinguish infrastructure failures from policy denials. However, ErrCapabilityCheckFailed is only included in the error chain for the IsInfraFailure() decision path (line 78). When the engine itself returns a Go error (the evalErr != nil branch, lines 57-60), the returned error wraps evalErr directly without ErrCapabilityCheckFailed in the chain. This means errors.Is(err, ErrCapabilityCheckFailed) returns false for engine panics, context cancellations forwarded as errors, or any other hard engine failure — contradicting the documented contract. Fix: add ErrCapabilityCheckFailed to the errors.Join call in the evalErr branch, mirroring the IsInfraFailure path. Example: Wrap(errors.Join(ErrCapabilityCheckFailed, evalErr)). No current callers rely on this sentinel (dispatcher.go checks the oops code instead), so there is no immediate breakage, but the broken contract is a maintenance hazard for future callers.","status":"open","priority":2,"issue_type":"bug","owner":"4678+seanb4t@users.noreply.github.com","created_at":"2026-03-10T16:12:42Z","created_by":"seanb4t","updated_at":"2026-03-10T16:12:42Z","external_ref":"https://github.com/holomush/holomush/pull/88"}

{"id":"holomush-gk8b.23","title":"Fix(holomush-gk8b.21): correct .mockery.yaml AccessPolicyEngine package paths","description":"Fix .mockery.yaml: remove AccessPolicyEngine from internal/world entry, change internal/access/policy to internal/access/policy/types","status":"open","priority":2,"issue_type":"task","owner":"4678+seanb4t@users.noreply.github.com","created_at":"2026-03-10T16:32:05Z","created_by":"seanb4t","updated_at":"2026-03-10T16:32:05Z"}

{"id":"holomush-gk8b.21","title":".mockery.yaml references wrong packages for AccessPolicyEngine — mockery regeneration will fail","description":"Two entries in .mockery.yaml point to incorrect packages after the AccessPolicyEngine interface was moved to internal/access/policy/types/types.go:

1. `github.com/holomush/holomush/internal/world` lists `AccessPolicyEngine` as an interface to generate. But the world package no longer defines this interface — the local AccessControl interface was removed and the code now imports from types. Running `mockery` will fail with 'interface not found'.

2. `github.com/holomush/holomush/internal/access/policy` lists `AccessPolicyEngine`. This interface was moved from engine.go (in the policy package) to types/types.go (in the types subpackage). Running `mockery` will also fail here.

Both mocks were pre-generated and committed in this PR, so builds and tests pass. But the config is stale and will break the next time anyone runs `mockery`.

Fix: Change both entries to reference `github.com/holomush/holomush/internal/access/policy/types` as the source package, with appropriate outpkg and dir settings.","status":"open","priority":1,"issue_type":"task","owner":"4678+seanb4t@users.noreply.github.com","created_at":"2026-03-10T16:13:58Z","created_by":"seanb4t","updated_at":"2026-03-10T16:13:58Z","external_ref":"https://github.com/holomush/holomush/pull/88"}
