{"id":"holomush-gk8b.24","title":"Fix(holomush-gk8b.18): add scene service engine-error tests","description":"Add engine-error and infra-failure test cases for AddSceneParticipant, RemoveSceneParticipant, ListSceneParticipants","status":"open","priority":2,"issue_type":"task","owner":"4678+seanb4t@users.noreply.github.com","created_at":"2026-03-10T16:32:07Z","created_by":"seanb4t","updated_at":"2026-03-10T16:32:07Z"}

{"id":"holomush-gk8b.18","title":"Scene service methods lack engine-error test coverage after ABAC migration","description":"The PR adds thorough engine-error tests (ErrAccessEvaluationFailed path) for Location, Exit, Object, Character, and MoveCharacter world service methods. However, the three scene methods that use checkAccess — AddSceneParticipant (line 629), RemoveSceneParticipant (line 650), and ListSceneParticipants (line 668) in internal/world/service.go — have no test cases covering the engine-error branch.

The existing scene tests in service_test.go (lines 1048–1609) cover authorization success and denial (ErrPermissionDenied), but none inject a policytest.NewErrorEngine() or policytest.NewInfraFailureEngine() to verify that:
- Engine failures return ErrAccessEvaluationFailed (not ErrPermissionDenied)
- The error code is SCENE_ACCESS_EVALUATION_FAILED (not SCENE_ACCESS_DENIED)
- Infrastructure failures are correctly classified

This is inconsistent with Location/Exit/Object which all received engine-error test coverage in this PR. A callers that errors.Is(err, world.ErrPermissionDenied) on a scene op may silently swallow what is actually a DB outage.

Suggested fix: Add sub-tests to TestWorldService_AddSceneParticipant, TestWorldService_RemoveSceneParticipant, and TestWorldService_ListSceneParticipants using policytest.NewErrorEngine() and policytest.NewInfraFailureEngine(), mirroring the pattern used in TestWorldService_GetLocation 'returns ErrAccessEvaluationFailed when engine errors' and 'returns ErrAccessEvaluationFailed on infrastructure failure'.","status":"open","priority":1,"issue_type":"task","owner":"4678+seanb4t@users.noreply.github.com","created_at":"2026-03-10T16:13:14Z","created_by":"seanb4t","updated_at":"2026-03-10T16:13:14Z","external_ref":"https://github.com/holomush/holomush/pull/88"}

{"id":"holomush-gk8b.25","title":"Fix(holomush-gk8b.17): add ErrCapabilityCheckFailed to evalErr branch","description":"Add ErrCapabilityCheckFailed sentinel to the evalErr branch in CheckCapability to match documented contract","status":"open","priority":2,"issue_type":"task","owner":"4678+seanb4t@users.noreply.github.com","created_at":"2026-03-10T16:32:09Z","created_by":"seanb4t","updated_at":"2026-03-10T16:32:09Z"}

{"id":"holomush-gk8b.20","title":"migration_equivalence_test skips assertion for 4 player-role read divergences with no follow-up tracking","description":"internal/access/migration_equivalence_test.go documents four player-role read cases as 'EXPECTED DIVERGENCE' and skips the equivalence assertion (lines 1560-1583 of the diff). The divergences are:
- player read other-character
- player read location
- player read object
- player read scene

These use t.Logf() instead of t.Fail() so a future regression (e.g. the new engine accidentally denying these when it should allow) would not be caught by this test suite.

The integration test test/integration/access/location_equivalence_test.go addresses location equivalence with a LocationResolver. However, the unit equivalence test provides no safety net if the policy engine's behavior changes for the other three resource types (object, scene, other-character).

Issues:
1. No assertion at all means the test cannot distinguish 'expected divergence where old=deny, new=allow' from 'unexpected regression where old=allow, new=deny'
2. The comment says 'EXPECTED DIVERGENCE: policy engine permits without location context' — this assumes new engine is more permissive. If that assumption breaks, no test catches it.

Suggested fix: Instead of t.Logf, assert the direction of the divergence explicitly:
  assert.True(t, policyResult, 'new engine should be more permissive than static for player reads without location context')
This would catch regressions where the new engine accidentally becomes MORE restrictive than the static engine.","status":"open","priority":2,"issue_type":"task","owner":"4678+seanb4t@users.noreply.github.com","created_at":"2026-03-10T16:13:39Z","created_by":"seanb4t","updated_at":"2026-03-10T16:13:39Z","external_ref":"https://github.com/holomush/holomush/pull/88"}

{"id":"holomush-gk8b.27","title":"Fix(holomush-gk8b.20): strengthen divergence assertions","description":"Change t.Logf to explicit direction assertions for the 4 expected divergences in migration_equivalence_test.go","status":"open","priority":2,"issue_type":"task","owner":"4678+seanb4t@users.noreply.github.com","created_at":"2026-03-10T16:32:22Z","created_by":"seanb4t","updated_at":"2026-03-10T16:32:22Z"}

{"id":"holomush-gk8b.34","title":"ErrAccessEvaluationFailed not joined into CheckCapability's infra-failure error path","description":"In internal/command/access.go CheckCapability (the command-layer function), the infra-failure branch at line 5148 wraps the error as:

  oops.Code(CodeAccessEvaluationFailed).Wrap(errors.Join(ErrCapabilityCheckFailed, errors.New(decision.Reason())))

This uses ErrCapabilityCheckFailed (command-layer sentinel), NOT ErrCapabilityCheckFailed joined with a reference sentinel that callers can use errors.Is for. The world-layer equivalent (world/service.go checkAccess) uses errors.Join(ErrAccessEvaluationFailed, err) so errors.Is(err, world.ErrAccessEvaluationFailed) works correctly.

The command-layer infra-failure path joins ErrCapabilityCheckFailed with errors.New(decision.Reason()). errors.New creates a new anonymous error, so errors.Is cannot unwrap to it. More critically, callers using errors.Is(err, ErrCapabilityCheckFailed) to detect command-layer infra failures CAN work, but there's no exported ErrCapabilityCheckFailed — wait, it IS exported (line 5095). So the sentinel chain works.

However, the eval-error path (when engine.Evaluate returns a Go error, line 5132) uses:
  oops.Code(CodeAccessEvaluationFailed).Wrap(evalErr)
This does NOT join ErrCapabilityCheckFailed, so errors.Is(err, ErrCapabilityCheckFailed) returns false for actual engine errors. Callers cannot use a single sentinel to detect ALL capability check infrastructure failures — they must check by oops code string instead.

This inconsistency between the two error paths (evalErr vs infra decision) makes the error contract asymmetric and fragile. If a caller upgrades to check by sentinel instead of string comparison, they would miss engine errors.

Suggested fix: Join ErrCapabilityCheckFailed in the evalErr path too:
  oops.Code(CodeAccessEvaluationFailed).Wrap(errors.Join(ErrCapabilityCheckFailed, evalErr))

File: internal/command/access.go line ~5132 in the diff.","status":"open","priority":1,"issue_type":"bug","owner":"4678+seanb4t@users.noreply.github.com","created_at":"2026-03-10T17:23:54Z","created_by":"seanb4t","updated_at":"2026-03-10T17:23:54Z","external_ref":"https://github.com/holomush/holomush/pull/88"}

{"id":"holomush-gk8b.31","title":"location_equivalence_test.go has Ginkgo Describe blocks outside the outer Describe closure","description":"In test/integration/access/location_equivalence_test.go, the 'Player self-write equivalence' Describe block and the 'Co-location character read' Describe block are placed OUTSIDE the outer var _ = Describe(...) closure. The file structure is:

  var _ = Describe("Location-based permission equivalence", func() {
      Describe("Player read access to current location", func() { ... })
  })                                          // <-- outer block closes here

  Describe("Player self-write equivalence", func() { ... })    // OUTSIDE outer block
  Describe("Co-location character read", func() { ... })       // OUTSIDE outer block

Top-level Describe() calls without var _ = Describe() are not valid Ginkgo top-level nodes — they need to be wrapped in var _ = Describe() or be nested. This will likely cause a compile error or Ginkgo will not register these specs correctly, meaning the self-write and co-location equivalence tests never run.

File: test/integration/access/location_equivalence_test.go, approximately line 328 (closing }) that prematurely ends the outer Describe).

This is a critical gap because the two Describe blocks outside the outer closure contain the only tests for:
- Player self-write equivalence (both engines agree on writing own character)
- Player writing other character denied (both engines agree on denial)

These are among the most important behavioral equivalences to verify during migration.","status":"open","priority":0,"issue_type":"bug","owner":"4678+seanb4t@users.noreply.github.com","created_at":"2026-03-10T17:23:29Z","created_by":"seanb4t","updated_at":"2026-03-10T17:23:29Z","external_ref":"https://github.com/holomush/holomush/pull/88"}

{"id":"holomush-gk8b.36","title":"CheckCapability infra failure wraps errors.New(decision.Reason()) into error chain","description":"internal/command/access.go:153: The infra failure path does:
  errors.Join(ErrCapabilityCheckFailed, errors.New(decision.Reason()))
This converts a human-readable reason string (e.g. 'session resolution failed') into a Go error value and splices it into the error chain. This is unusual Go idiom: errors.Is/As chains exist for typed sentinel matching, not for string reasons. The reason is already available via decision.Reason() and is included in oops context via With('reason', ...). Wrapping it as a bare errors.New value adds no structural benefit and could mislead callers doing errors.Is checks.

Suggestion: Remove the errors.New(decision.Reason()) and just wrap ErrCapabilityCheckFailed:
  Wrap(ErrCapabilityCheckFailed)

The reason is already in the oops context fields, which is the correct place for it per project error handling conventions.","status":"open","priority":2,"issue_type":"task","owner":"4678+seanb4t@users.noreply.github.com","created_at":"2026-03-10T17:27:05Z","created_by":"seanb4t","updated_at":"2026-03-10T17:27:05Z","external_ref":"https://github.com/holomush/holomush/pull/88"}

{"id":"holomush-gk8b.32","title":"canExecuteCommand circuit breaker trips at 3rd error but skips remainder of commands silently from user perspective","description":"In internal/plugin/hostfunc/commands.go, the canExecuteCommand circuit breaker (const maxEngineErrors = 3) trips after 3 engine failures. When it does, it breaks out of the loop early, leaving any remaining commands in the registry unevaluated.

The break at line 11811 correctly sets hadEngineError = true (since hadError is already true from the prior iteration). The outer loop then exits, and the Lua result includes incomplete=true and the error string — so the user is notified.

However, the circuit breaker log message at line 11807 only logs engine_failures and threshold. It does not log how many commands were skipped (commands remaining vs commands evaluated), making it harder to quantify the blast radius of a degraded engine in production.

More importantly: the break exits after the 3rd failure without appending the current command to filtered even if it might have been allowed. Since canExecuteCommand returns (false, true) on error (fail-closed), the current command is not appended regardless. But commands AFTER the circuit break point receive no evaluation at all — they are also excluded even if the engine recovers, and the user has no way to know which commands were excluded vs which they lack permissions for (only that 'some' are missing).

This is by design (fail-closed) and the incomplete flag surfaces it. The suggestion is to log the count of skipped commands at the break point for observability.

File: internal/plugin/hostfunc/commands.go around line 11807 in the diff.","status":"open","priority":2,"issue_type":"task","owner":"4678+seanb4t@users.noreply.github.com","created_at":"2026-03-10T17:23:39Z","created_by":"seanb4t","updated_at":"2026-03-10T17:23:39Z","external_ref":"https://github.com/holomush/holomush/pull/88"}

{"id":"holomush-gk8b.30","title":"PlayerMessage gap: world-layer ACCESS_EVALUATION_FAILED codes fall to default case","description":"The PR introduces world-layer error codes like LOCATION_ACCESS_EVALUATION_FAILED, EXIT_ACCESS_EVALUATION_FAILED, OBJECT_ACCESS_EVALUATION_FAILED, CHARACTER_ACCESS_EVALUATION_FAILED, and EXAMINE_ACCESS_EVALUATION_FAILED (from checkAccess in internal/world/service.go:13330). These codes are preserved and propagated up through handlers (look.go, move.go, objects.go) to the command dispatcher, which calls PlayerMessage().

However, PlayerMessage() in internal/command/errors.go:154 only handles the command-layer code CodeAccessEvaluationFailed ("ACCESS_EVALUATION_FAILED"). The world-layer codes like LOCATION_ACCESS_EVALUATION_FAILED do not match any case and fall to the default branch (line 193), which logs a slog.Warn("unhandled error code in PlayerMessage") and returns the generic "Something went wrong. Try again." message.

This has two problems:
1. The user receives the generic fallback message instead of the intended "Permission check failed. Please try again or contact an administrator." message — so the user cannot distinguish a transient engine failure from any other system error.
2. A slog.Warn fires on every single world-service engine failure that reaches a player-facing command (look, move, objects set) — creating log noise in production proportional to failure rate.

The look.go, move.go handlers explicitly preserve these codes with: if errors.Is(err, world.ErrAccessEvaluationFailed) { return err }
That correctly avoids wrapping as WORLD_ERROR, but then the code still doesn't match PlayerMessage's switch.

Suggested fix: either add cases for each world-layer *_ACCESS_EVALUATION_FAILED code in PlayerMessage, or map them via errors.Is(err, world.ErrAccessEvaluationFailed) using oops sentinel unwrapping before the switch, or normalize to ACCESS_EVALUATION_FAILED at the handler boundary before returning.","status":"open","priority":1,"issue_type":"bug","owner":"4678+seanb4t@users.noreply.github.com","created_at":"2026-03-10T17:23:24Z","created_by":"seanb4t","updated_at":"2026-03-10T17:23:24Z","external_ref":"https://github.com/holomush/holomush/pull/88"}

{"id":"holomush-gk8b.33","title":"Boot handler success tests no longer verify which specific capability is checked","description":"Prior to this PR, TestBootHandler_Success, TestBootHandler_SuccessWithReason, TestBootHandler_CaseInsensitiveMatch, TestBootHandler_SkipsInaccessibleCharacters, TestBootHandler_EndSessionError, and TestBootHandler_LogsUnexpectedGetCharacterErrors each explicitly asserted:

{"description":"In internal/access/migration_equivalence_test.go, several player-role read operations are documented as 'EXPECTED DIVERGENCE' — the static engine denies (location context required via $here token) while the new policy engine allows. The test asserts the direction of divergence but not the specific behavior.

The test comment says 'Location-based permission equivalence is validated separately in production integration tests where LocationResolver is available' — pointing to test/integration/access/location_equivalence_test.go (//go:build integration).

CONFIRMED: .github/workflows/ci.yaml does NOT run integration tests. Integration tests require a database (testcontainers) and are not part of the standard PR CI job.

This means the documented divergence in migration_equivalence_test.go is NOT validated by CI. If the location-based equivalence is wrong, it would only be caught by manually running 'task test:integration'.

Risk: Low for an initial migration, but if the player role permissions diverge in unexpected ways for location-scoped reads (look, examine, who commands), users could see behavior changes.

Recommendation: Either (1) add a Taskfile CI step that runs access integration tests with testcontainers on PR, or (2) add a comment to the equivalence test explicitly noting that integration tests must be run manually before merge, or (3) extract a subset of location equivalence tests that can run without a real DB using a stub LocationResolver."}

{"id":"holomush-gk8b.43","title":"Session resolution denials not emitted to audit log","description":"In internal/access/policy/engine.go, the session resolution failure paths (both SESSION_INVALID and session-store-error) return (decision, nil) without emitting an audit log entry. Every other denial path in Evaluate now correctly calls e.audit.Log(ctx, entry) before returning, but the session branch (Step 2, lines ~88-101 in the new code) exits via 'return decision, nil' with no audit entry.

This means:
- Failed session resolutions are invisible to the audit trail
- Operators cannot distinguish a burst of session failures from quiet normal traffic
- SESSION_INVALID denials (which may indicate session spoofing or replay attacks) produce no audit record

The slog.ErrorContext call added for session-store-error provides structured logging, but the dedicated audit.Logger is a separate persistence/forwarding path (e.g., database, SIEM) that is bypassed.

Fix: add an audit.Entry construction and e.audit.Log(ctx, entry) call immediately before the 'return decision, nil' in the session failure branch, mirroring the pattern used in the stale-cache and no-candidates paths.

File: internal/access/policy/engine.go, Step 2 session resolution error handler.","status":"open","priority":1,"issue_type":"bug","owner":"4678+seanb4t@users.noreply.github.com","created_at":"2026-03-10T18:45:54Z","created_by":"seanb4t","updated_at":"2026-03-10T18:45:54Z","external_ref":"https://github.com/holomush/holomush/pull/88"}

{"id":"holomush-gk8b.45","title":"Test code bypasses access.CharacterResource() helper with raw string concatenation","description":"The PR introduces access.CharacterSubject() and access.CharacterResource() helpers specifically to centralize prefix construction and guard against empty IDs. However, new test code in boot_test.go bypasses these helpers:

**Files:** internal/command/handlers/boot_test.go (lines ~6413-6414, ~6468-6469, ~6554-6555)

**Pattern in new code:**
```go
accessControl.Grant(access.SubjectCharacter+executorID.String(), "read", "character:"+executorID.String())
worldEngine.Grant(access.SubjectCharacter+executorID.String(), "read", "character:"+targetID.String())
```

**Should be:**
```go
accessControl.Grant(access.CharacterSubject(executorID.String()), "read", access.CharacterResource(executorID.String()))
worldEngine.Grant(access.CharacterSubject(executorID.String()), "read", access.CharacterResource(targetID.String()))
```

The subject side uses raw constant concatenation (access.SubjectCharacter + id) instead of calling access.CharacterSubject(id), and the resource side uses literal string concatenation ("character:"+id) instead of access.CharacterResource(id). This is inconsistent with the PR's stated goal of eliminating scattered string concatenation patterns, and bypasses the empty-ID panic guards on CharacterSubject() and CharacterResource(). While the IDs are guaranteed non-empty from ulid.Make() in these tests, the inconsistency sets a precedent that future tests may copy.

**Confidence:** 82","status":"open","priority":2,"issue_type":"task","owner":"4678+seanb4t@users.noreply.github.com","created_at":"2026-03-10T18:47:46Z","created_by":"seanb4t","updated_at":"2026-03-10T18:47:46Z","external_ref":"https://github.com/holomush/holomush/pull/88"}

{"id":"holomush-gk8b.47","title":"maxEngineErrors circuit breaker threshold duplicated across packages without shared constant","description":"The circuit breaker threshold value 3 for engine errors is defined independently in two places:

1. internal/command/handlers/who.go (line ~9527): package-level const maxEngineErrors = 3
2. internal/plugin/hostfunc/commands.go (inside listCommandsFn): const maxEngineErrors = 3

These represent the same concept (max engine failures before aborting) but are not shared. If the threshold needs tuning (e.g., in response to observing production behavior), both locations must be updated separately with no compiler enforcement.

**Suggestion:** Extract to a shared constant, either in internal/access/policy/types or in a dedicated package. Alternatively, document that these are intentionally independent thresholds (e.g., WhoHandler is per-session list, listCommandsFn is per-command-list) and add a comment explaining the divergence.

Recommendation: Either (1) add a Taskfile CI step that runs access integration tests with testcontainers on PR, or (2) add a comment to the equivalence test explicitly noting that integration tests must be run manually before merge, or (3) extract a subset of location equivalence tests that can run without a real DB using a stub LocationResolver.","status":"open","priority":2,"issue_type":"task","owner":"4678+seanb4t@users.noreply.github.com","created_at":"2026-03-10T18:49:38Z","created_by":"seanb4t","updated_at":"2026-03-10T18:50:31Z","external_ref":"https://github.com/holomush/holomush/pull/88"}

{"id":"holomush-gk8b.61","title":"Migration equivalence test documents but does not assert player read-permission divergence","description":"internal/access/migration_equivalence_test.go (lines 1560-1582) contains four test cases documented as 'EXPECTED DIVERGENCE: static engine uses $here token requiring LocationResolver; policy engine permits without location context'. These cases are in the shared test table and will execute, but TestMigrationEquivalence compares both engines and asserts equivalence for ALL rows. The divergent cases will either fail (if static returns deny and new returns allow) or they are skipped by some guard -- the test code around line 1600 onward needs checking. The concern is: if these cases are included in the assertEqual loop without special handling, they will fail; if they are excluded, the PR has no test validating that the divergence is intentional and bounded. The comment says location-based equivalence is 'validated separately in production integration tests where LocationResolver is available' but no corresponding integration test file name is cited. Recommend: (1) verify the divergent cases are actually skipped or have a dedicated branch in the test loop, and (2) add a reference to the specific integration test that covers this divergence (test/integration/access/location_equivalence_test.go appears to exist per the diff at line 22912 -- confirm it covers the player read-location case).","status":"open","priority":2,"issue_type":"task","owner":"4678+seanb4t@users.noreply.github.com","created_at":"2026-03-10T19:44:37Z","created_by":"seanb4t","updated_at":"2026-03-10T19:44:37Z","external_ref":"https://github.com/holomush/holomush/pull/88"}

{"id":"holomush-gk8b.65","title":"TestBootHandler_BootOthers_IncludesDecisionContext uses policytest.NewGrantEngine for boot capability check but DenyAllEngine for Services.Engine — asymmetry could mask test failures","description":"In internal/command/handlers/boot_test.go around line 6218-6432, the test TestBootHandler_BootOthers_WithoutCapability (and similar tests) builds a world service with policytest.NewGrantEngine() for character read access but passes policytest.DenyAllEngine() as Services.Engine for the boot capability check. This is intentional to test the 'no admin.boot capability' path.\\n\\nHowever, the policytest.NewGrantEngine() is a different instance from Services.Engine, yet both are set on different injection points (world.ServiceConfig.Engine vs. command.ServicesConfig.Engine). This is correct since world and command engines are separate. The concern is subtle: if a test bug causes the grant engine to be swapped with deny, the test may pass vacuously because the character lookup silently fails and the command returns the wrong error type. The test assertions (line ~6228: assert.Contains(t, PlayerMessage(err), 'permission')) may be too weak to detect this — 'system error' would also contain PlayerMessage fallback text.\\n\\nSuggested improvement: assert specifically on CodePermissionDenied rather than a substring match, to make it impossible for ACCESS_EVALUATION_FAILED to pass the assertion as a false positive.","status":"open","priority":2,"issue_type":"task","owner":"4678+seanb4t@users.noreply.github.com","created_at":"2026-03-10T19:52:17Z","created_by":"seanb4t","updated_at":"2026-03-10T19:52:17Z","external_ref":"https://github.com/holomush/holomush/pull/88"}

{"id":"holomush-gk8b.57","title":"Entity-scoped *_ACCESS_EVALUATION_FAILED codes are unhandled in PlayerMessage, triggering 'unhandled error code' warning","description":"The checkAccess helper in internal/world/service.go generates entity-scoped error codes such as LOCATION_ACCESS_EVALUATION_FAILED, EXIT_ACCESS_EVALUATION_FAILED, OBJECT_ACCESS_EVALUATION_FAILED, SCENE_ACCESS_EVALUATION_FAILED, CHARACTER_ACCESS_EVALUATION_FAILED, and EXAMINE_ACCESS_EVALUATION_FAILED. When these errors propagate out of command handlers (look.go, move.go, handlers/objects.go, etc.) they reach PlayerMessage() in internal/command/errors.go. The switch in PlayerMessage only handles the command-level CodeAccessEvaluationFailed (= 'ACCESS_EVALUATION_FAILED'). The entity-level codes fall through to the default branch, which fires slog.Warn('unhandled error code in PlayerMessage', ...) and returns the generic 'Something went wrong. Try again.' message. This produces misleading warn-level log noise in production (these are not unhandled — they are intentional engine failures) and gives the player no indication it is a transient infrastructure failure versus some other error. Fix: either (a) add cases for the entity-level evaluation-failed codes in PlayerMessage returning the same 'Permission check failed. Please try again or contact an administrator.' message, or (b) switch to errors.Is(err, world.ErrAccessEvaluationFailed) as a pre-switch check before the oops code switch.","status":"open","priority":2,"issue_type":"task","owner":"4678+seanb4t@users.noreply.github.com","created_at":"2026-03-10T19:43:53Z","created_by":"seanb4t","updated_at":"2026-03-10T19:43:53Z","external_ref":"https://github.com/holomush/holomush/pull/88"}

{"id":"holomush-3cm7.12","title":"TestCheckCapability is located in handlers package rather than command package","description":"CheckCapability() is defined in internal/command/access.go (package command), but its primary unit tests (TestCheckCapability, TestCheckCapability_EvalErr_IncludesErrCapabilityCheckFailed) live in internal/command/handlers/boot_test.go (package handlers). This placement is incidental — the tests were added in the boot handler file because boot.go was the first caller — rather than alongside the function definition.

File: internal/command/handlers/boot_test.go lines 1343-1487 (TestCheckCapability in wrong package)
File: internal/command/access.go (function being tested)

The tests work correctly across the package boundary, but a dedicated internal/command/access_test.go would:
1. Make it obvious where to find CheckCapability tests
2. Avoid the tests being silently skipped if boot_test.go is excluded
3. Follow the project convention of foo.go -> foo_test.go

This is a test organization issue, not a coverage gap, but can mislead future contributors looking for CheckCapability coverage.","status":"open","priority":2,"issue_type":"task","owner":"4678+seanb4t@users.noreply.github.com","created_at":"2026-03-10T20:40:01Z","created_by":"seanb4t","updated_at":"2026-03-10T20:40:01Z","external_ref":"https://github.com/holomush/holomush/pull/88"}

{"id":"holomush-3cm7.4","title":"who handler circuit breaker counts skipped sessions before logging the trip event","description":"In internal/command/handlers/who.go (~line 39-52 of the new code), the circuit breaker check at the top of the loop increments errorCount by len(sessions)-i before breaking — but the WarnContext log about the circuit breaker tripping is only emitted when engineErrorCount reaches the threshold on the error-incrementing path below (around line 46-52). This means: if engineErrorCount hits the threshold on iteration N, the trip log fires for that iteration. On the NEXT iteration, errorCount is bulk-incremented by len(sessions)-i via the early-exit guard, but no log is emitted for those skipped sessions. The operator sees a 'circuit breaker tripped' log mentioning skipped_sessions=K, but the actual final errorCount reported to the user (via the '(Note: N players could not be displayed)' message) includes additional skips from the bulk-increment that are not reflected in the log. This is a minor observability gap — the log and the user-facing count can disagree. Suggested fix: log the total skipped count (via the bulk-increment) at the break site, or log once after the loop completes showing final engineErrorCount and errorCount.","status":"open","priority":2,"issue_type":"bug","owner":"4678+seanb4t@users.noreply.github.com","created_at":"2026-03-10T20:37:19Z","created_by":"seanb4t","updated_at":"2026-03-10T20:37:19Z","external_ref":"https://github.com/holomush/holomush/pull/88"}

{"id":"holomush-3cm7.2","title":"hasBypass infra-failure surfaced via bare oops.Errorf loses original decision context","description":"In internal/command/rate_limit_middleware.go hasBypass() (~line 80-82 of the new code), when a decision.IsInfraFailure() is detected, the function returns a synthesized error: oops.Errorf(\"infrastructure failure during bypass check: %s\", decision.Reason()). This converts the structured Decision (which carries policy_id and reason as typed fields) into a plain string-formatted error. The Enforce() caller then logs this as a generic 'rate limit bypass check failed' warn log. As a result: (1) the policy_id is lost from the log, making it harder to correlate with audit logs; (2) observability.RecordEngineFailure is called without the infra failure being distinguishable from a Go-level engine error. Suggested fix: return a structured oops error that preserves decision.PolicyID() and decision.Reason() as With() context fields rather than embedding them in the message string. This mirrors the pattern used in CheckCapability in internal/command/access.go which does oops.Code(...).With(\"reason\", decision.Reason()).With(\"policy_id\", decision.PolicyID()).","status":"open","priority":2,"issue_type":"bug","owner":"4678+seanb4t@users.noreply.github.com","created_at":"2026-03-10T20:36:57Z","created_by":"seanb4t","updated_at":"2026-03-10T20:36:57Z","external_ref":"https://github.com/holomush/holomush/pull/88"}

{"id":"holomush-3cm7.3","title":"CreateLocation and CreateExit use wildcard resource strings bypassing access.LocationResource/ExitResource guards","description":"In internal/world/service.go, CreateLocation (~line 118) and CreateExit (~line 168) call s.checkAccess with literal wildcard strings 'location:*' and 'exit:*' respectively, bypassing the new access.LocationResource() and access.ExitResource() helper functions. These helpers panic on empty IDs to prevent bypass, but wildcard resources are intentional for create operations and don't have IDs yet. However, this means the wildcard strings are hardcoded literals in two places rather than using a named constant or helper. If the prefix ever changes (e.g. during a future migration), these literals could silently diverge from the prefix constants defined in access/prefix.go. Similarly, CreateObject uses 'object:*' and FindLocationByName uses 'location:*'. Suggested fix: define access.WildcardResource(prefix string) string helper or per-type constants (e.g. access.LocationWildcard = access.ResourceLocation + '*') for use in create/list operations so the prefix is not duplicated as a string literal. This is a maintainability concern, not a current bug, but given the security sensitivity of this code it merits attention.","status":"open","priority":2,"issue_type":"bug","owner":"4678+seanb4t@users.noreply.github.com","created_at":"2026-03-10T20:37:08Z","created_by":"seanb4t","updated_at":"2026-03-10T20:37:08Z","external_ref":"https://github.com/holomush/holomush/pull/88"}

{"id":"holomush-3cm7.14","title":"Fix(holomush-3cm7.5): slog.Error to slog.ErrorContext in subscriber.go","description":"Change slog.Error to slog.ErrorContext(tctx,...) at subscriber.go emit error log","status":"open","priority":2,"issue_type":"task","owner":"4678+seanb4t@users.noreply.github.com","created_at":"2026-03-10T22:13:16Z","created_by":"seanb4t","updated_at":"2026-03-10T22:13:16Z"}

{"id":"holomush-3cm7.5","title":"subscriber.go context leak: original ctx used after tctx timeout fires for plugin emit","description":"In internal/plugin/subscriber.go deliverAsync(), the PR correctly moves context.WithTimeout inside the goroutine to fix a pre-existing context leak (the old code created tctx/cancel before the goroutine, meaning the cancel was deferred in the calling scope and the timeout window started before the goroutine executed). The fix uses tctx correctly for DeliverEvent. However, at ~line 135, emitter.EmitPluginEvent is also called with tctx. If the plugin execution takes close to the 5-second limit, tctx may already be expired or near-expiry when EmitPluginEvent is called. If EmitPluginEvent blocks (e.g. writing to a full channel or waiting for DB), it will immediately fail with context.DeadlineExceeded, and the error is logged as 'failed to emit plugin event'. This is correct fail-visible behavior, but the error log at that point uses slog.Error (not slog.ErrorContext) — it loses the trace context embedded in tctx. Suggested fix: use slog.ErrorContext(tctx, ...) at the emit error log site so trace IDs propagate to the error log. This is a minor logging quality issue, not a silent failure.","status":"open","priority":1,"issue_type":"bug","owner":"4678+seanb4t@users.noreply.github.com","created_at":"2026-03-10T20:37:30Z","created_by":"seanb4t","updated_at":"2026-03-10T20:37:30Z","external_ref":"https://github.com/holomush/holomush/pull/88"}

{"id":"holomush-3cm7.15","title":"Fix(holomush-3cm7.1): resolver error Warn to Error in engine.go","description":"Change slog.WarnContext to slog.ErrorContext at engine.go line 130 for resolver error aggregation","status":"open","priority":2,"issue_type":"task","owner":"4678+seanb4t@users.noreply.github.com","created_at":"2026-03-10T22:13:16Z","created_by":"seanb4t","updated_at":"2026-03-10T22:13:16Z"}

{"id":"holomush-3cm7.16","title":"Fix(holomush-3cm7.8): add resolver error fail-closed test to engine_test.go","description":"Add test that injects mock attribute provider returning error and asserts engine.Evaluate returns error with zero-value Decision","status":"open","priority":2,"issue_type":"task","owner":"4678+seanb4t@users.noreply.github.com","created_at":"2026-03-10T22:13:17Z","created_by":"seanb4t","updated_at":"2026-03-10T22:13:17Z"}

{"id":"holomush-3cm7.1","title":"Attribute resolver errors cause fail-closed denial without logging at call-site in engine","description":"In internal/access/policy/engine.go (Step 3, ~line 287-295), when resolver.Resolve returns a non-nil error (from a failing attribute provider), the engine returns a zero-value Decision and the wrapped error. This is correct fail-closed behavior, BUT the error is logged only at slog.Warn level with label 'attribute resolution failed — fail-closed'. For an event that causes every subsequent access check to fail (e.g., a DB outage affecting the attribute provider), this will produce a high-volume stream of Warn logs. More critically, the WarnContext call includes the error but not the Decision; callers see an empty Decision{} (EffectDefaultDeny, allowed=false) but the code comment says 'Callers treat non-nil error as denial' — the Decision itself is the zero value and IsAllowed()=false, which is correct. The concern is: the log level is Warn rather than Error for what is effectively an infra outage path. Consider escalating to slog.ErrorContext to ensure on-call alerting fires. Also note the resolver itself logs at Error level per provider failure, but the engine-level aggregation log is Warn — the severity mismatch could confuse operators. Suggested fix: use slog.ErrorContext at the engine aggregation point in engine.go ~line 289 to match the severity of the underlying resolver errors.","status":"open","priority":1,"issue_type":"bug","owner":"4678+seanb4t@users.noreply.github.com","created_at":"2026-03-10T20:36:46Z","created_by":"seanb4t","updated_at":"2026-03-10T20:36:46Z","external_ref":"https://github.com/holomush/holomush/pull/88"}

{"id":"holomush-3cm7.11","title":"Session resolution deny path in engine.go skips RecordEvaluationMetrics — infra denials invisible to metrics","description":"In internal/access/policy/engine.go, Step 2 (session resolution) returns early on error with a deny Decision and nil error. This is correct per the contract, but the early return skips RecordEvaluationMetrics. Every other deny path (stale cache, no candidates, combined deny) calls RecordEvaluationMetrics before returning. Session-related infra failures will not appear in evaluation metrics, making it harder to detect degraded session stores via dashboards.\n\nFix: Add RecordEvaluationMetrics(time.Since(start), decision.Effect()) before the return in the session resolution error branch (around line 116-117 in engine.go).","status":"open","priority":2,"issue_type":"bug","owner":"4678+seanb4t@users.noreply.github.com","created_at":"2026-03-10T20:39:55Z","created_by":"seanb4t","updated_at":"2026-03-10T20:39:55Z","external_ref":"https://github.com/holomush/holomush/pull/88"}

{"id":"holomush-3cm7.7","title":"PlayerMessage: *_ACCESS_DENIED codes from world service produce generic error message instead of permission denied","description":"In internal/command/handlers/look.go, move.go, and objects.go the error guard only checks errors.Is(err, world.ErrAccessEvaluationFailed). When checkAccess in world/service.go produces a policy denial (ErrPermissionDenied wrapped as LOCATION_ACCESS_DENIED / OBJECT_ACCESS_DENIED / EXIT_ACCESS_DENIED etc.), the handler falls through to: oops.Code(command.CodeWorldError).Wrap(err). PlayerMessage handles CodeWorldError by showing oopsErr.Context()["message"], but checkAccess sets no 'message' context on denial errors. The result is the player sees 'Something went wrong. Try again.' for permission denials on read/move/set operations, not 'You don't have permission to do that.'

Fix options:
1. Add errors.Is(err, world.ErrPermissionDenied) guard alongside ErrAccessEvaluationFailed in each handler (consistent with objects.go SetHandler which already does this correctly).
2. Or add a HasSuffix(code, "_ACCESS_DENIED") check in PlayerMessage similar to the existing _ACCESS_EVALUATION_FAILED suffix check.

Note: SetHandler in objects.go already correctly handles both ErrPermissionDenied and ErrAccessEvaluationFailed, but LookHandler, MoveHandler, createObject, and createLocation do not.","status":"open","priority":1,"issue_type":"bug","owner":"4678+seanb4t@users.noreply.github.com","created_at":"2026-03-10T20:39:34Z","created_by":"seanb4t","updated_at":"2026-03-10T20:39:34Z","external_ref":"https://github.com/holomush/holomush/pull/88"}

{"id":"holomush-3cm7.13","title":"Migration equivalence test skips location-based player permission paths without integration coverage","description":"TestMigrationEquivalence in internal/access/migration_equivalence_test.go documents several EXPECTED DIVERGENCE cases for player read permissions (read character, read location, read object, read scene) because the static engine uses $here token location-based access which requires a LocationResolver unavailable in the unit test.

File: internal/access/migration_equivalence_test.go lines 401-428 (EXPECTED DIVERGENCE cases)

The test correctly documents these as known divergences where the new policy engine is more permissive (allows without location context). The comment says 'Location-based permission equivalence is validated separately in production integration tests where LocationResolver is available.'

However, test/integration/access/location_equivalence_test.go exists but should be verified to cover these specific divergence scenarios: player reading a character, location, object, and scene where the static engine would deny (no location resolver) but the policy engine allows. If these cases are not explicitly asserted in the integration test, the documented divergences are only tested in one direction (confirming static denies but not confirming policy allows match actual player experience).

A regression here would mean players could lose read access to locations and objects after migration completes in Phase 7.7, with no failing test to catch it.","status":"open","priority":2,"issue_type":"task","owner":"4678+seanb4t@users.noreply.github.com","created_at":"2026-03-10T20:40:14Z","created_by":"seanb4t","updated_at":"2026-03-10T20:40:14Z","external_ref":"https://github.com/holomush/holomush/pull/88"}

{"id":"holomush-3cm7.10","title":"PlayerMessage suffix-matching branch for world-service access failures has no unit test","description":"The PR added a new branch in PlayerMessage() in internal/command/errors.go (lines 157-162) that handles entity-scoped access evaluation failure codes (e.g., LOCATION_ACCESS_EVALUATION_FAILED, CHARACTER_ACCESS_EVALUATION_FAILED) using strings.HasSuffix(code, "_ACCESS_EVALUATION_FAILED"). This branch is the primary path through which world.Service checkAccess failures surface to players.

File: internal/command/errors.go lines 157-162 (the HasSuffix branch)
File: internal/command/errors_test.go TestPlayerMessage (missing cases)

The existing TestPlayerMessage test in errors_test.go covers CodeAccessEvaluationFailed (command layer) but does NOT exercise the new suffix-matching branch. The following codes are untested in PlayerMessage:
- "LOCATION_ACCESS_EVALUATION_FAILED"
- "EXIT_ACCESS_EVALUATION_FAILED"  
- "CHARACTER_ACCESS_EVALUATION_FAILED"
- "OBJECT_ACCESS_EVALUATION_FAILED"
- "SCENE_ACCESS_EVALUATION_FAILED"

If the HasSuffix check were accidentally removed or changed, no test would catch it. Players would get 'Something went wrong. Try again.' instead of the correct 'Permission check failed. Please try again or contact an administrator.' message.

Suggested fix: Add test cases to TestPlayerMessage covering at least one entity-specific suffix code, e.g.:
  {name: "location access evaluation failed", err: oops.Code("LOCATION_ACCESS_EVALUATION_FAILED").Errorf("engine error"), expected: "Permission check failed. Please try again or contact an administrator."}","status":"open","priority":2,"issue_type":"task","owner":"4678+seanb4t@users.noreply.github.com","created_at":"2026-03-10T20:39:50Z","created_by":"seanb4t","updated_at":"2026-03-10T20:39:50Z","external_ref":"https://github.com/holomush/holomush/pull/88"}

{"id":"holomush-3cm7.6","title":"checkAccess entityPrefix parameter is caller-controlled — wrong prefix silently miscodes errors","description":"In internal/world/service.go, the checkAccess helper accepts a free-form entityPrefix string (e.g. 'LOCATION', 'EXIT', 'EXAMINE') which is used to construct error codes like 'LOCATION_ACCESS_EVALUATION_FAILED'. The prefix is entirely caller-controlled and not validated. Two issues arise: (1) ExamineLocation, ExamineObject, and ExamineCharacter all pass 'EXAMINE' as the entity prefix, producing codes like 'EXAMINE_ACCESS_EVALUATION_FAILED'. The PlayerMessage function in internal/command/errors.go handles these via the strings.HasSuffix(code, '_ACCESS_EVALUATION_FAILED') check, so user-facing messages are correct. However, the error codes 'EXAMINE_ACCESS_DENIED' and 'EXAMINE_ACCESS_EVALUATION_FAILED' do not match the per-entity pattern (LOCATION_ACCESS_DENIED, CHARACTER_ACCESS_DENIED) used everywhere else, making metrics and error monitoring inconsistent. (2) There is no compile-time check that a given prefix is valid. A typo like 'LOACTION' would produce 'LOACTION_ACCESS_DENIED' — a code never caught by PlayerMessage's suffix check (since it ends in '_ACCESS_DENIED', not '_ACCESS_EVALUATION_FAILED'), falling through to the default 'Something went wrong. Try again.' Suggested fix: define prefix constants (e.g. const prefixLocation = 'LOCATION') and require callers to use them, or validate the prefix against a known set. For the Examine* methods, pass the target entity type ('LOCATION', 'CHARACTER', 'OBJECT') rather than the operation name.","status":"open","priority":1,"issue_type":"bug","owner":"4678+seanb4t@users.noreply.github.com","created_at":"2026-03-10T20:37:42Z","created_by":"seanb4t","updated_at":"2026-03-10T20:37:42Z","external_ref":"https://github.com/holomush/holomush/pull/88"}

{"id":"holomush-3cm7.8","title":"engine_test.go lacks coverage for fail-closed resolver error path (Step 3)","description":"The PR changed engine.go Step 3 from silently swallowing resolver.Resolve() errors (bags = NewAttributeBags()) to returning them as hard failures (fail-closed). This is a breaking behavioral change with no corresponding test in engine_test.go.

File: internal/access/policy/engine_test.go (no test for resolveErr path)
File: internal/access/policy/engine.go lines 104-122 (the changed behavior)

The test gap: when resolver.Resolve() returns a non-nil error, Evaluate() now returns (Decision{}, error) instead of continuing with empty bags. There is no test in engine_test.go that injects a resolver error and asserts:
1. Evaluate() returns an error
2. The returned Decision is zero-value (denied)
3. Metrics record an evaluation failure

Without this test, a future refactor could silently revert to the old permissive behavior (evaluating policies against empty attribute bags rather than denying).

The resolver_test.go does cover the Resolve() error paths, and migration_equivalence_test.go covers end-to-end behavior, but engine_test.go's unit isolation of this path is missing. A minimal test would inject a mock attribute provider that returns an error and assert that engine.Evaluate() propagates it.","status":"open","priority":1,"issue_type":"bug","owner":"4678+seanb4t@users.noreply.github.com","created_at":"2026-03-10T20:39:38Z","created_by":"seanb4t","updated_at":"2026-03-10T20:39:38Z","external_ref":"https://github.com/holomush/holomush/pull/88"}

{"description":"The PR migrates GetCharactersByLocation from the legacy compound resource (location:<id>:characters, action=read) to the decomposed form (resource=location:<id>, action=list_characters) per ADR #76. The service-level test TestWorldService_GetCharactersByLocation_VerifiesAccessRequest (service_test.go:18825) correctly asserts action==list_characters.

However, the migration equivalence test (internal/access/migration_equivalence_test.go) has NO case for the list_characters action -- not for admin, builder, or any role. This means there is no verification that the new ABAC policy engine actually grants list_characters to appropriate roles, only that the world service passes the correct action string to the engine.

This is a semantic behavioral gap: if the new policies were never seeded with list_characters grants, all GetCharactersByLocation calls would be silently denied in production. The equivalence test is the right place to catch this.

Suggested fix: Add cases to TestMigrationEquivalence for admin and builder roles with action=list_characters. Also verify createCacheWithEquivalentPolicies() includes a list_characters grant.

File: internal/access/migration_equivalence_test.go"}

{"id":"holomush-0hag.8","title":"GetCharactersByLocation action decomposition (list_characters) absent from migration equivalence test","description":"The PR migrates GetCharactersByLocation from the legacy compound resource (location:<id>:characters, action=read) to the decomposed form (resource=location:<id>, action=list_characters) per ADR #76 documented in the service.go comment.

The migration equivalence test (internal/access/migration_equivalence_test.go) intentionally skips player read operations due to LocationResolver dependency, but it also has NO case testing the list_characters action at all — not even for admin or builder roles. This means the ADR-mandated semantic change is unvalidated by any equivalence assertion.

This is significant because:
- The static engine would check the action=read against location:<id>:characters (a compound resource)
- The new engine checks action=list_characters against location:<id>
- These are fundamentally different authorization semantics
- If the new policies do not grant list_characters, all character list operations will silently fail or be denied in production

Suggested fix: Add test cases to TestMigrationEquivalence for admin and builder roles:
  - {subject: "character:admin-01ABC", action: "list_characters", resource: "location:01JKL"}
  - {subject: "character:builder-01GHI", action: "list_characters", resource: "location:01JKL"}

Also add a test in TestWorldService_GetCharactersByLocation that explicitly confirms the new action string is list_characters (TestWorldService_GetCharactersByLocation_VerifiesAccessRequest already exists at line 18825 — verify it asserts action=="list_characters", not "read").

{"id":"holomush-0hag.1","title":"PlayerMessage silently falls through for entity-scoped _ACCESS_DENIED codes","description":"SetHandler (objects.go:167-175) and the propertyQuerier/propertyMutator helpers return world.ErrPermissionDenied errors directly when errors.Is(err, world.ErrPermissionDenied) matches. These errors carry entity-scoped oops codes such as LOCATION_ACCESS_DENIED, CHARACTER_ACCESS_DENIED, OBJECT_ACCESS_DENIED, EXIT_ACCESS_DENIED, EXAMINE_ACCESS_DENIED produced by world/service.go checkAccess().

When PlayerMessage() processes these errors, the suffix check only matches '_ACCESS_EVALUATION_FAILED', and the switch case only matches exact 'PERMISSION_DENIED'. Entity-scoped '_ACCESS_DENIED' codes fall through to the default case which: (1) logs 'unhandled error code in PlayerMessage' as a slog.Warn, and (2) returns the generic 'Something went wrong. Try again.' message instead of 'You don't have permission to do that.'

Affected call sites: internal/command/handlers/objects.go SetHandler (line ~173), propertyQuerier.GetLocation (~283), propertyQuerier.GetObject (~299), propertyMutator.UpdateLocation (~328), propertyMutator.UpdateObject (~345).

Fix options:
1. Add a second suffix check: if strings.HasSuffix(code, "_ACCESS_DENIED") { return "You don't have permission to do that." }
2. Or map these errors to CodePermissionDenied before returning them from SetHandler.
3. Or add a separate case for each entity-scoped code (verbose, not preferred).","status":"open","priority":1,"issue_type":"bug","owner":"4678+seanb4t@users.noreply.github.com","created_at":"2026-03-10T23:05:54Z","created_by":"seanb4t","updated_at":"2026-03-10T23:05:54Z","external_ref":"https://github.com/holomush/holomush/pull/88"}

{"id":"holomush-0hag.3","title":"who.go circuit breaker log fires before break — skipped_sessions count off-by-one scenario","description":"In internal/command/handlers/who.go WhoHandler, when the engine error count hits maxEngineErrors (3), a warning is logged with 'skipped_sessions': len(sessions)-i-1. This is the count of sessions *after* the current one (index i). The break does not happen until the next iteration's guard check.

The logged skipped_sessions value of len(sessions)-i-1 excludes the current session (which was already counted as errorCount++). This is accurate.

However there is a subtle inconsistency: the log message says 'aborting after engine failures' but the actual abort happens on the NEXT iteration (at the top-of-loop guard). The circuit breaker log message correctly captures the state at threshold, but a reader debugging the log may be confused that processing continues for the rest of the current iteration (the 'continue' on line 9724 skips to the next element).

This is a suggestion only — the behavior is correct and fail-closed. The log would be clearer if accompanied by a comment explaining the deferred break pattern, or if the log were moved to the top-of-loop guard instead of the threshold detection point.","status":"open","priority":2,"issue_type":"task","owner":"4678+seanb4t@users.noreply.github.com","created_at":"2026-03-10T23:06:17Z","created_by":"seanb4t","updated_at":"2026-03-10T23:06:17Z","external_ref":"https://github.com/holomush/holomush/pull/88"}

{"id":"holomush-0hag.7","title":"canExecuteCommand() IsInfraFailure decision path untested in hostfunc/commands_test.go","description":"In internal/plugin/hostfunc/commands.go, canExecuteCommand() has three distinct error paths after Evaluate() is called:
1. err != nil (Go error) → hadError=true, return false
2. !decision.IsAllowed() && decision.IsInfraFailure() → hadError=true, return false
3. !decision.IsAllowed() && !decision.IsInfraFailure() → hadError=false, return false

Path 2 (infra failure decision) is not tested. TestListCommands_EngineError_HidesCapabilityCommands only covers path 1 (Go error from engine). The listCommandsFn caller only increments engineErrorCount when hadError==true, meaning an infra failure decision should also trigger the circuit breaker and mark the result as incomplete — but this is not verified.

The missing test should use policytest.NewInfraFailureEngine() to simulate a decision where IsInfraFailure()==true and verify:
- The command requiring that capability is excluded (fail-closed)
- incomplete is set to true in the result table
- engineErrorCount is incremented (circuit breaker behavior)

{"id":"holomush-0hag.8","title":"GetCharactersByLocation action decomposition (list_characters) absent from migration equivalence test","description":"The PR migrates GetCharactersByLocation from the legacy compound resource (location:<id>:characters, action=read) to the decomposed form (resource=location:<id>, action=list_characters) per ADR #76. The service-level test TestWorldService_GetCharactersByLocation_VerifiesAccessRequest (service_test.go:18825) correctly asserts action==list_characters.

However, the migration equivalence test (internal/access/migration_equivalence_test.go) has NO case for the list_characters action -- not for admin, builder, or any role. This means there is no verification that the new ABAC policy engine actually grants list_characters to appropriate roles, only that the world service passes the correct action string to the engine.

This is a semantic behavioral gap: if the new policies were never seeded with list_characters grants, all GetCharactersByLocation calls would be silently denied in production. The equivalence test is the right place to catch this.

Suggested fix: Add cases to TestMigrationEquivalence for admin and builder roles with action=list_characters. Also verify createCacheWithEquivalentPolicies() includes a list_characters grant.

{"id":"holomush-0hag.2","title":"engine.go attribute resolution error returns zero Decision without infra: policy ID","description":"In internal/access/policy/engine.go Evaluate(), when attribute resolution fails (line ~2493), the code returns: types.Decision{}, oops.With(...).Wrap(resolveErr)

The returned Decision is the zero value: effect=0 (EffectDefaultDeny), policyID="", allowed=false. Since policyID is empty, IsInfraFailure() returns false (no 'infra:' prefix).

The interface contract (types.go, AccessPolicyEngine doc comment) states: 'on error paths the returned Decision is always the zero value (EffectDefaultDeny, allowed=false)'. This is correct from a fail-closed standpoint — callers check err != nil first. However there is an observability gap: because the Decision has no 'infra:' policyID, any caller that logs decision.PolicyID() or decision.Reason() before checking err will see empty strings, making debugging harder.

More critically: the world/service.go checkAccess() and command/access.go CheckCapability() both correctly check err != nil first and do not inspect the Decision on error. So the security invariant is maintained.

The suggestion is to return a proper infra-failure Decision alongside the error for better observability and debuggability, e.g.:
  d := types.NewDecision(types.EffectDefaultDeny, "attribute resolution failed", "infra:attribute-resolution-failed")
  return d, oops.With(...).Wrap(resolveErr)

This would also make the returned value consistent with the stale-cache path which returns a Decision with policyID 'infra:policy-cache-stale'.","status":"open","priority":1,"issue_type":"bug","owner":"4678+seanb4t@users.noreply.github.com","created_at":"2026-03-10T23:06:07Z","created_by":"seanb4t","updated_at":"2026-03-10T23:06:07Z","external_ref":"https://github.com/holomush/holomush/pull/88"}

{"id":"holomush-0hag.17","title":"GetCharactersByLocation uses prefixCharacter for a location-resource access check","description":"In internal/world/service.go, GetCharactersByLocation calls:

  s.checkAccess(ctx, subjectID, "list_characters", resource, prefixCharacter)

where resource = access.LocationResource(locationID.String()) (i.e., "location:<id>").

The entityPrefix parameter determines the error code prefix. Using prefixCharacter here causes the error codes to be CHARACTER_ACCESS_DENIED and CHARACTER_ACCESS_EVALUATION_FAILED when the underlying resource is a location and the action is list_characters.

This is misleading for:
- Operators reading logs ("CHARACTER_ACCESS_DENIED" but the subject is querying a location)
- Metrics (RecordEngineFailure keyed on "CHARACTER_access_check")
- PlayerMessage() suffix matching: _ACCESS_DENIED suffix handling works, but the wrong code is emitted

The ADR #76 decomposition intent is to use resource=location:<id> with action=list_characters. The error prefix should follow the resource type (LOCATION) not the thing being listed (CHARACTER).

Fix: Change the call to use prefixLocation instead of prefixCharacter:

  if err := s.checkAccess(ctx, subjectID, "list_characters", resource, prefixLocation); err != nil {","status":"open","priority":2,"issue_type":"bug","owner":"4678+seanb4t@users.noreply.github.com","created_at":"2026-03-11T00:06:21Z","created_by":"seanb4t","updated_at":"2026-03-11T00:06:21Z","external_ref":"https://github.com/holomush/holomush/pull/88"}

{"id":"holomush-0hag.19","title":"Fix(holomush-0hag.17): change prefixCharacter to prefixLocation in GetCharactersByLocation","description":"Fix entity prefix in GetCharactersByLocation checkAccess call in service.go","status":"open","priority":2,"issue_type":"task","owner":"4678+seanb4t@users.noreply.github.com","created_at":"2026-03-11T00:10:31Z","created_by":"seanb4t","updated_at":"2026-03-11T00:10:31Z"}

{"id":"holomush-0hag.14","title":"AccessPolicyEngine interface doc contradicts implementation: non-zero Decision returned alongside error","description":"The AccessPolicyEngine interface docstring in internal/access/policy/types/types.go (~line 1041) states: 'The engine never returns a permissive Decision alongside an error; on error paths the returned Decision is always the zero value (EffectDefaultDeny, allowed=false).'

However, the Engine.Evaluate implementation in internal/access/policy/engine.go (~line 615) contradicts this: on attribute resolution failure it creates a non-zero Decision via NewDecision(EffectDefaultDeny, 'attribute resolution failed', 'infra:attribute-resolution-failed') and returns it with a non-nil error. The zero value of Decision has effect=0 (EffectDefaultDeny) and allowed=false, so the security property holds, but the returned Decision carries a non-empty Reason and PolicyID — it is not the zero value.

Any caller that inspects decision.Reason() or decision.PolicyID() when err != nil may see misleading data. More importantly, the contract comment creates a false belief that callers can safely ignore the Decision when err != nil. If any future caller conditionally inspects the decision on error paths expecting zero-value semantics, they may behave unexpectedly.

Suggestion: either update the interface doc to accurately reflect that a deny-effect non-zero Decision may be returned alongside a non-nil error, or change the implementation to always return types.Decision{} when returning an error.","status":"open","priority":2,"issue_type":"task","owner":"4678+seanb4t@users.noreply.github.com","created_at":"2026-03-11T00:05:48Z","created_by":"seanb4t","updated_at":"2026-03-11T00:05:48Z","external_ref":"https://github.com/holomush/holomush/pull/88"}

{"id":"holomush-0hag.20","title":"Fix(holomush-0hag.16): rename cache_export.go to export_test.go","description":"Rename cache_export.go to export_test.go so test helpers are not compiled into production binary","status":"open","priority":2,"issue_type":"task","owner":"4678+seanb4t@users.noreply.github.com","created_at":"2026-03-11T00:10:32Z","created_by":"seanb4t","updated_at":"2026-03-11T00:10:32Z"}

{"id":"holomush-0hag.16","title":"cache_export.go ships test helpers in production package binary","description":"internal/access/policy/cache_export.go is a regular Go source file (not a _test.go file), so its contents — NewCacheWithPoliciesForTest and NewCacheWithPoliciesForTest — are compiled into production binaries. The doc comment explicitly states this is 'intended for use in migration equivalence tests and other tests'.

In Go, the idiomatic way to expose unexported package members for tests is to name the file export_test.go (package policy), not cache_export.go. A file named export_test.go is only compiled during test builds and is invisible to importers.

The current placement means production binaries export a test helper that directly mutates internal cache mutex state, which adds unnecessary surface area and binary size.

Fix: Rename internal/access/policy/cache_export.go to internal/access/policy/export_test.go. The package declaration stays 'package policy' (not 'package policy_test'), which is the correct idiom for a white-box test export file.","status":"open","priority":2,"issue_type":"task","owner":"4678+seanb4t@users.noreply.github.com","created_at":"2026-03-11T00:06:10Z","created_by":"seanb4t","updated_at":"2026-03-11T00:06:10Z","external_ref":"https://github.com/holomush/holomush/pull/88"}

{"id":"holomush-c3ww.9","title":"oops.Wrap may not propagate context.Canceled through errors.Join for errors.Is traversal","description":"internal/world/service_helpers_test.go line 14493 asserts errors.Is(err, context.Canceled) where err was returned from checkAccess wrapping as oops.Code(failCode).Wrap(errors.Join(ErrAccessEvaluationFailed, context.Canceled)). This requires that (a) oops.Wrap preserves the errors.Join unwrap chain and (b) errors.Is can traverse through both the oops wrapper and the errors.Join tree. The oops library's Wrap method may not implement the Unwrap() []error interface needed for errors.Join trees — it typically only exposes Unwrap() error (single). If oops.Wrap only unwraps one level to the errors.Join node, and then errors.Is doesn't know to unwrap the Join's children, context.Canceled would not be findable. The test at line 14493 would then fail in CI. The same pattern appears in internal/command/access.go line 5373 (errors.Join(ErrCapabilityCheckFailed, evalErr)) and world/service.go line 13980 (errors.Join(ErrAccessEvaluationFailed, reqErr)). This should be verified by running the test, or the wrapping should use a pattern known to work with errors.Is: either oops.Wrap(err) alone (with ErrAccessEvaluationFailed checked separately) or a custom error type. {"id":"holomush-c3ww.10","title":"TestCheckCapability infra-failure case uses ad-hoc name switch instead of test field","description":"In internal/command/access_test.go, the table-driven TestCheckCapability test at line ~5476 detects the 'infra failure' case via a switch on tt.name (case tt.name == \"infra failure — returns ACCESS_EVALUATION_FAILED\"), rather than a dedicated bool field. {"id":"holomush-c3ww.3","title":"who.go circuit breaker logs warning one iteration after threshold is reached","description":"In internal/command/handlers/who.go, the circuit breaker fires at the TOP of the loop when engineErrorCount >= maxEngineErrors, but the log message (and increment that hits the threshold) fires INSIDE the ErrAccessEvaluationFailed block. The result is: on the Nth failure (N = maxEngineErrors = 3), the warn log fires inside the block ('circuit breaker tripped: aborting after engine failures'), then the loop continues to the next iteration where the break fires. This means one more session is started (and the top-of-loop check fires before any work is done), but more importantly the log message says 'skipped_sessions: len(sessions)-i-1' which is computed at the point the threshold is hit, not when the break actually fires. So skipped_sessions is off by one—it reports N-1 fewer skips than actually occurred.

For example with 10 sessions and maxEngineErrors=3: on session index 2 (3rd failure), the log fires with skipped_sessions = 10-2-1 = 7. Then at session index 3, the break fires. Actual skipped sessions = 6 (indices 4-9), not 7.

Suggested fix: Move the warning log to just before or just after the break statement, computing skipped_sessions as len(sessions)-i.

{"id":"holomush-c3ww.7","title":"TestCheckAccess uses raw string literals for entityPrefix typed parameter","description":"internal/world/service_helpers_test.go (e.g., lines 14430, 14443, 14474, 14489) calls svc.checkAccess(..., 'LOCATION') and svc.checkAccess(..., 'EXIT') with untyped string literals where the parameter type is entityPrefix. This compiles because untyped string constants are assignable to named string types, but it bypasses the purpose of entityPrefix: the typed prefix prevents typos by restricting allowed values to the declared constants (prefixLocation, prefixExit, etc.). The tests should use the declared constants (prefixLocation, prefixExit) to enforce that callers use the proper typed API. Since the test file is in package world (not world_test), it has direct access to the unexported constants. Files: internal/world/service_helpers_test.go lines 14430, 14443, 14474, 14489, 14494.","status":"open","priority":2,"issue_type":"task","owner":"4678+seanb4t@users.noreply.github.com","created_at":"2026-03-11T01:15:13Z","created_by":"seanb4t","updated_at":"2026-03-11T01:15:13Z","external_ref":"https://github.com/holomush/holomush/pull/88"}

{"id":"holomush-c3ww.15","title":"Fix(.3): Move circuit breaker log to break point in who.go","description":"Fix off-by-one in who.go circuit breaker: move the warning log to just before the break statement, computing skipped_sessions as len(sessions)-i.","status":"open","priority":2,"issue_type":"task","owner":"4678+seanb4t@users.noreply.github.com","created_at":"2026-03-11T01:34:10Z","created_by":"seanb4t","updated_at":"2026-03-11T01:34:10Z"}

{"id":"holomush-c3ww.4","title":"migration equivalence test hard-codes divergence direction without verifying it","description":"In internal/access/migration_equivalence_test.go, the divergence-handling branch (lines 1703-1709) unconditionally asserts staticResult==false AND policyResult==true for every test case that has a non-empty comment. This means the test will silently pass even if the new engine unexpectedly DENIES what the comment claims it should allow, as long as the assert.True fails softly (not require.True). More critically, it hardcodes the assumption that ALL documented divergences are 'new engine is MORE permissive' — but some divergences (e.g., list_characters which is a brand-new action with no equivalent in the static engine) may legitimately fail policyEngine.Evaluate with an error rather than returning IsAllowed()==true. The test uses require.NoError on the policy engine evaluation (line 1693), so if the engine returns an error for an unknown action, the entire test case panics/fails rather than documenting the divergence gracefully. Fix: use require.True instead of assert.True for the divergence assertions so failures are non-deferred, and add a third divergence category for new-action cases where the static engine denies AND the policy engine may also deny (with a different reason). File: internal/access/migration_equivalence_test.go lines ~1703-1709.","status":"open","priority":1,"issue_type":"bug","owner":"4678+seanb4t@users.noreply.github.com","created_at":"2026-03-11T01:14:38Z","created_by":"seanb4t","updated_at":"2026-03-11T01:14:38Z","external_ref":"https://github.com/holomush/holomush/pull/88"}

{"id":"holomush-c3ww.13","title":"TestMigrationEquivalence divergence assertions test direction but not error type","description":"In internal/access/migration_equivalence_test.go, the documented-divergence branch at line ~1705 asserts that staticResult is false and policyResult is true (new engine is more permissive). However, the EXPECTED DIVERGENCE comment fields for list_characters cases describe a *new* decomposed action not in the static engine — which is correct behavior, not a regression. The concern is that there is no test case for the inverse divergence direction: a case where the policy engine is MORE restrictive than the static engine. If such a regression were introduced, the equivalence test would not catch it (it only asserts strict equality for non-divergent cases and a specific direction for documented divergent cases). Consider adding at least a comment explaining why all documented divergences are new-engine-more-permissive and will never need the inverse direction, or add a negative divergence test case to validate the assertion logic itself.","status":"open","priority":2,"issue_type":"task","owner":"4678+seanb4t@users.noreply.github.com","created_at":"2026-03-11T01:17:03Z","created_by":"seanb4t","updated_at":"2026-03-11T01:17:03Z","external_ref":"https://github.com/holomush/holomush/pull/88"}

{"id":"holomush-c3ww.5","title":"ErrNilEngine sentinel is shared between Dispatcher and RateLimitMiddleware, hiding which constructor failed","description":"internal/command/errors.go defines a single ErrNilEngine sentinel (line 6599) shared between NewDispatcher and NewRateLimitMiddleware. The comment explicitly says 'callers should not distinguish which constructor failed based on this error alone,' but this makes it impossible for callers wrapping both constructors to emit specific error messages or metrics. If a caller creates a Dispatcher (success) and then passes that engine to NewRateLimitMiddleware (failure), both failures look identical. The CLAUDE.md idiom 'errors are values — handle them explicitly' is better served by separate sentinel errors (ErrNilDispatcherEngine, ErrNilRateLimiterEngine) or by including the constructor name in the oops context. Low severity since the current design is intentional and documented, but the comment 'callers should not distinguish' is a code smell. Files: internal/command/errors.go:6599, internal/command/dispatcher.go:5583, internal/command/rate_limit_middleware.go:5088.","status":"open","priority":2,"issue_type":"task","owner":"4678+seanb4t@users.noreply.github.com","created_at":"2026-03-11T01:14:48Z","created_by":"seanb4t","updated_at":"2026-03-11T01:14:48Z","external_ref":"https://github.com/holomush/holomush/pull/88"}

{"id":"holomush-c3ww.6","title":"canExecuteCommand circuit breaker fires AFTER adding hadEngineError to filtered commands","description":"In internal/plugin/hostfunc/commands.go listCommandsFn (lines 12423-12440), when engineErrorCount reaches maxEngineErrors (3), the loop breaks AFTER the current command has already been processed (allowed=false, hadError=true are recorded for the command that hit the threshold). However the check at line 12429 fires the circuit breaker BEFORE processing the next command, which is correct. But canExecuteCommand (lines 12494-12526) returns immediately on the first capability error for a command, so if a command has multiple capabilities and the engine fails on capability 2 out of 3, the remaining capabilities 3 are not checked. This is intentional (fail-closed), but the outer loop then increments engineErrorCount for that ONE command failure, not once per command. A single command with many capabilities that all fail will trip the circuit breaker in 3 iterations of the INNER loop across potentially just 1-2 commands of the OUTER loop. This is likely the intended behavior but the comment 'After this many total engine errors' is misleading — it's actually 'after this many per-capability engine errors.' The circuit breaker documentation should clarify this, or the threshold logic should track per-command errors. Files: internal/plugin/hostfunc/commands.go lines 12419-12440.","status":"open","priority":1,"issue_type":"bug","owner":"4678+seanb4t@users.noreply.github.com","created_at":"2026-03-11T01:15:02Z","created_by":"seanb4t","updated_at":"2026-03-11T01:15:02Z","external_ref":"https://github.com/holomush/holomush/pull/88"}

{"id":"holomush-c3ww.12","title":"MoveCharacter and DeleteCharacter world service methods lack infra-failure path tests","description":"TestWorldService_MoveCharacter, TestWorldService_DeleteCharacter, TestWorldService_ExamineLocation, TestWorldService_ExamineObject, and TestWorldService_ExamineCharacter in internal/world/service_test.go all cover allow, default-deny, and engine-error paths, and some have explicit-deny (DenyAllEngine) subtests, but none have an infra-failure path test (using policytest.NewInfraFailureEngine). By contrast, RemoveSceneParticipant, ListSceneParticipants, and GetCharactersByLocation have the complete trio. The infra-failure path through checkAccess triggers a distinct branch (IsInfraFailure()) that produces ErrAccessEvaluationFailed instead of ErrPermissionDenied — a behavioral distinction that callers depend on to avoid mis-classifying transient failures as policy denials. The unit tests in service_helpers_test.go do cover the infra branch at the checkAccess level, so the risk is partially mitigated, but per-method integration at the service layer is still worth protecting. Suggested fix: add t.Run('returns ErrAccessEvaluationFailed on infrastructure failure') subtests for MoveCharacter, DeleteCharacter, ExamineLocation, ExamineObject, and ExamineCharacter using policytest.NewInfraFailureEngine.","status":"open","priority":2,"issue_type":"task","owner":"4678+seanb4t@users.noreply.github.com","created_at":"2026-03-11T01:16:53Z","created_by":"seanb4t","updated_at":"2026-03-11T01:17:44Z","external_ref":"https://github.com/holomush/holomush/pull/88"}

{"id":"holomush-c3ww.17","title":"Fix(.12): Add infra-failure subtests for 5 world service methods","description":"Add InfraFailureEngine subtests for MoveCharacter, DeleteCharacter, ExamineLocation, ExamineObject, ExamineCharacter","status":"open","priority":2,"issue_type":"task","owner":"4678+seanb4t@users.noreply.github.com","created_at":"2026-03-11T01:42:42Z","created_by":"seanb4t","updated_at":"2026-03-11T01:42:42Z"}

{"id":"holomush-c3ww.2","title":"Rate limit bypass engine error dropped without propagation","description":"In internal/command/rate_limit_middleware.go, RateLimitMiddleware.Enforce logs the engine error at Warn level and records a failure metric, but then drops the error entirely and falls through to the rate limiting check. This is the documented fail-closed behavior (apply rate limiting rather than grant bypass), but the error is not propagated to the caller. The result is that a persistent engine outage during rate limit bypass checks produces Warn log entries and metrics, but the Enforce return value is either nil (rate limit not exceeded) or ErrRateLimited (rate limit exceeded)—the caller at the dispatcher level never knows that the bypass check itself failed. This is distinct from the access evaluation failures on capability checks (which do surface as errors).

The fail-closed behavior is correct. The suggestion is to either:
1. Return an explicit error (e.g., ErrCapabilityCheckFailed wrapped) when the bypass check fails, distinct from ErrRateLimited, so callers can differentiate 'rate limited' from 'bypass check failed'.
2. At minimum, add a comment explaining why the error is intentionally dropped here (there is a comment saying 'fail-closed' but it doesn't explain why the error isn't returned).

File: internal/command/rate_limit_middleware.go (~line 56-63)","status":"open","priority":2,"issue_type":"task","owner":"4678+seanb4t@users.noreply.github.com","created_at":"2026-03-11T01:14:08Z","created_by":"seanb4t","updated_at":"2026-03-11T01:14:08Z","external_ref":"https://github.com/holomush/holomush/pull/88"}

{"id":"holomush-c3ww.22","title":"audit Logger.Log() always returns nil, preventing callers from detecting write failures","description":"In internal/access/policy/audit/logger.go, the Log() method returns nil in all code paths (lines 139 and 149), even when both the primary WriteSync and the WAL fallback have failed.

File: internal/access/policy/audit/logger.go:115-151

Problem: The Logger.Log() signature returns error, but the implementation always returns nil:
- Line 139: after sync write + WAL both fail, slog.Error is called but the function returns nil
- Line 149: after async channel full (entry dropped), returns nil

This makes the '_ = auditErr' pattern in engine.go look like a justified blank discard when in reality the Logger has already handled and swallowed the error internally. The return value of Log() provides no useful signal, but its error return type implies it should.

Two options:
1. Change the Logger.Log() signature to return nothing (if callers should never need to act on it)
2. Return a real error from Log() when both DB and WAL fail (critical failure), letting the caller decide whether to surface it

Option 2 is preferable for audit integrity: a write that fails both DB and WAL for a denial should be surfaced as a real error to the engine so it can be logged at the engine level (fixing the engine.go '_ = auditErr' sites as a consequence).","status":"open","priority":2,"issue_type":"task","owner":"4678+seanb4t@users.noreply.github.com","created_at":"2026-03-11T02:07:22Z","created_by":"seanb4t","updated_at":"2026-03-11T02:07:22Z","external_ref":"https://github.com/holomush/holomush/pull/88"}

{"id":"holomush-c3ww.28","title":"Fix(holomush-c3ww.22): Return error from Log() on critical failure","description":"Make audit Logger.Log() return a real error when both primary write AND WAL fallback fail. Keep returning nil for async channel-full drops.","status":"open","priority":2,"issue_type":"task","owner":"4678+seanb4t@users.noreply.github.com","created_at":"2026-03-11T02:14:00Z","created_by":"seanb4t","updated_at":"2026-03-11T02:14:00Z"}
